candidates.summary: Nurse anesthetist, small-business owner, Navy veteran, and Democratic Party activist mounting an intra-party challenge to the incumbent. She centers healthcare access and argues the legislature needs a nurse's frontline perspective.
highlights: Registered nurse for over 25 years and CRNA; owns a practice providing anesthesia care, including gender-affirming, abortion, and reproductive care [S1]
Argues no nurses currently serve in the legislature and a nurse's problem-solving perspective is needed in Olympia [S1]
Campaign priorities are cost of living, healthcare access, and defending human dignity against federal government actions [S2]
Dual-endorsed by King County Democrats alongside the incumbent [S5]; her campaign site also lists The Stranger, Stonewall Democrats, and nursing organizations [S2]
Larger small-donor base than the incumbent by contribution count (177 vs. 75 records) at roughly 40% of his dollar total [S3]

experience.basis: Runs explicitly on a frontline-practitioner fresh perspective against a five-year incumbent, though she holds party roles (PCO, DNC delegate, former LD chair) rather than a pure outsider profile.

social.basis: Professionally provides gender-affirming, abortion, and reproductive care; Pro-Choice Washington PAC member with Stonewall Democrats and Planned Parenthood endorsement claims.

spending.basis: Supports affordable housing, K-12 investments, and career training as levers for working families squeezed by costs — an invest-and-expand orientation.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 27 (also edition-2 page 36)

candidates.name: Christian Rombough
candidates.summary: Army veteran and journeyman union ironworker running as the only Republican in the race. His pitch is anti-Olympia accountability, local control, and public safety, paired with a pledge to defend trade unions.
highlights: Argues Olympia has grown too wasteful and disconnected; pledges to cut red tape, demand accountability, restore local control, and back public safety [S1]
Union ironworker (Ironworkers Local 86) who says he will defend trade unions, an unusual pairing with his Republican party preference [S1]
Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge supporting an Article V convention resolution for congressional term limits [S3]

spending.basis: Explicit pamphlet statement that Olympia is 'too wasteful, too arrogant'; pledges to cut red tape and demand accountability before anything else.

local-control.basis: Explicitly campaigns on restoring local control as a core plank of his pamphlet statement.

safety.basis: Pledges to back public safety within an anti-Olympia accountability platform; enforcement-leaning framing but no specific policy detail.

reform.basis: Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge committing to support an Article V convention resolution for congressional term limits.

experience.basis: No elected experience listed; runs a full outsider case against a 'disconnected' Olympia from a tradesman-veteran profile.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 27 (also edition-2 page 36)

candidates.name: David Hackney
candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative serving since 2021, an attorney and former federal and UN war crimes prosecutor who chairs the Washington State Legislative Black Caucus. He runs on his legislative record and a cost-of-living agenda.
highlights: Incumbent since 2021; Vice Chair of the House Consumer Protection & Business Committee and chair of the Legislative Black Caucus [S3]
States his singular focus is lowering costs for everyday people, from housing to healthcare [S1]
Prime sponsor of HB 1149 (2025) targeting organized animal fighting, signed into law after near-unanimous passage [S5]
Has prioritized clean energy initiatives and environmental justice for his constituents [S4]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times [S8] and Washington State Labor Council [S10], with Governor Ferguson and AG Nick Brown per his pamphlet [S1]; dual-endorsed by King County Democrats [S9]

experience.basis: Five years in the seat with a committee vice-chairmanship, Black Caucus leadership, and enacted legislation — the maximal proven-record profile in this race.

climate.basis: Has prioritized clean energy initiatives and made environmental justice and air quality a top priority per legislative record and the Seattle Times endorsement.

spending.basis: Genuinely mixed: campaigns on bold state-led investment to drive down costs, while listing improving state government performance and protecting taxpayers among 2026 session priorities.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 11th Legislative District (Renton, Tukwila, and parts of south Seattle/south King County), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Steve Bergquist is the only candidate who filed, so he advances from the primary automatically and faces no listed opponent.

candidates.name: Steve Bergquist
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative and active Renton high-school teacher, one of the only classroom teachers serving in the legislature; running unopposed.
highlights: Serves on the House Education and Appropriations committees while teaching at Lindbergh High School in Renton [S1][S2]
Cites passage of HB 1587 college promise scholarships as a signature accomplishment [S1]
Wants to expand childcare, preschool (Ready K), and college affordability programs, and to help create more housing near jobs [S1]

experience.basis: Long-serving incumbent with education-focused committee assignments and a cited legislative record (HB 1587).

spending.basis: Platform centers on expanding public programs: childcare, preschool, and college promise scholarships.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 28 (same statement as edition-2 page 37)

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 12th Legislative District (all of Chelan County plus parts of Snohomish and King counties after 2024 redistricting), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: First-term Republican incumbent Brian Burnett, a former three-term Chelan County Sheriff, faces Democrat Stacy Willoughby, a Chelan bank branch manager and anti-poverty nonprofit board president. With only two candidates, both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Brian Burnett
candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state representative first elected in 2024, previously elected Chelan County Sheriff for three terms after more than 25 years in law enforcement.
highlights: Past president of the Washington Association of Sheriffs & Police Chiefs; platform leads with reducing crime and "victim-centered justice that holds criminals accountable" [S1][S2]
Names parental rights and returning decision-making to local communities as priorities [S1]
No endorsements listed in his pamphlet statement or retrieved campaign-site content [S1][S3]

safety.basis: 25+ year law-enforcement career, three terms as elected sheriff, and a platform of crime reduction and holding criminals accountable.

social.basis: Names protecting "your role as a parent" as a core plank, without further policy detail.

spending.basis: Fiscal plank is prioritizing taxpayer dollars and reducing burdens on small businesses — a restraint framing.

local-control.basis: Explicit plank on returning decision-making to local communities.

experience.basis: Twelve years as an elected sheriff plus one legislative term — a proven-elected-record profile, though with a short legislative tenure and no bills cited.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 38
WA House Republicans member page

sources.note: homepage returned no substantive content beyond title

candidates.name: Stacy Willoughby
candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate from Chelan with 25 years in financial services, currently a bank branch manager and board president of the rent-assistance nonprofit Chelan Valley Hope.
highlights: Would champion a state budget prioritizing "tax fairness for rural families and small businesses" [S1][S2]
Emphasizes affordable housing that serves each community and trades training for youth [S1]
Endorsed by the 12th LD Democrats, King County Democrats, Washington State Labor Council, and AFT-Washington [S1]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, running on financial-services and nonprofit leadership against an incumbent former sheriff.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 38

sources.note: homepage returned no substantive content beyond title

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Five-term Republican incumbent Mike Steele faces a well-funded intra-party challenge from Wenatchee pastor Adam James, who has out-raised him roughly four-to-one, while Democrat Maggie Adams of Snoqualmie is the only candidate from the district's King County side. In this cross-Cascades district a Republican-vs-Republican general election is possible under the top-two primary.

candidates.name: Mike Steele
candidates.summary: Five-term incumbent Republican from Chelan, chamber-of-commerce director, and nine-year ranking member of the House Capital Budget Committee who runs on a bipartisan-dealmaker record.
highlights: Says the state has a spending problem, not a revenue problem; favors lower taxes, less red tape, and balanced budgets [S2][S4]
Ranking member of the House Capital Budget Committee for nine years; cites over $32 million secured for district projects in the 2026 supplemental capital budget [S3][S4]
Opposes decriminalization and emphasizes personal responsibility for offenders alongside improving officer-community relationships [S4]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, the Washington State Medical Association, and the House Republican Organizing Committee [S4]
Wants to move K-12 toward hands-on, competency-based learning and expanded career/technical training [S2][S4]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly campaigns on lower taxes, cutting B&O tax burdens, and the view that the state has a spending problem, not a revenue problem.

spending.basis: Explicitly frames the budget as a spending problem and favors balanced budgets protecting taxpayers before new spending.

safety.basis: Opposes decriminalization and stresses offender accountability, but pairs it with improving officer-community relationships rather than a maximal enforcement platform.

experience.basis: Five-term incumbent, past Deputy Minority Leader, nine years as Capital Budget ranking member with an enacted record.

healthcare.basis: Wants to expand affordable options via price transparency and cost control within existing market structures; no public-system transformation agenda.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 39
PDC contributions dataset (data.wa.gov kv7h-kjye), election_year 2026, filer "Michael A. Steele (Mike Steele)

candidates.name: Adam James
candidates.summary: Wenatchee pastor and private Christian school co-founder with no prior elected office, challenging the incumbent from the right and leading the race in fundraising.
highlights: Platform of tax cuts, deregulation, and reducing state spending via government-efficiency measures [S2][S3]
Backs voter ID, proof of citizenship, and reform of the mail-in ballot system [S2][S3]
Runs as 'tough on crime,' opposing catch-and-release approaches and sanctuary-state policies [S2][S3]
Supports expanded school choice, 'parental empowerment,' and defense of traditional family structures, framing his candidacy as 'bold, Christian, and conservative leadership' [S2][S3]
Endorsed by the King, Snohomish, and Douglas county Republican Party organizations against the sitting Republican; dominant fundraiser at $343,869 [S1][S3][S6]

taxes.basis: Economy plank centers on tax cuts and deregulation.

spending.basis: Wants to reduce state spending through government-efficiency measures.

housing.basis: Prescribes permitting reform and easing requirements to lower home prices, with incentives for $200,000-or-less homes; a supply/deregulation approach.

safety.basis: Explicitly 'tough on crime,' opposes catch-and-release, backs law enforcement, and wants a different approach to homelessness and addiction.

experience.basis: No prior elected experience; runs as a movement-conservative outsider against a ten-year incumbent.

immigration.basis: Opposes sanctuary-state policies as part of his enforcement plank.

social.basis: School choice, 'parental empowerment,' opposition to 'ideological indoctrination,' and defense of traditional family structures are central campaign themes.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 39
PDC contributions dataset (data.wa.gov kv7h-kjye), election_year 2026, filer "Adam T. James (Adam James)

candidates.name: Maggie Adams
candidates.summary: Snoqualmie-based IATSE union business representative and professional stage worker, the only Democrat in the race and the only candidate from the district's King County side.
highlights: Supports a millionaire's tax and higher taxes on high-grossing businesses to fund tax fairness and inflation relief [S1][S3]
Views accessible, comprehensive healthcare as a right and wants to expand medical access across all regions of the state [S2][S3]
Would regulate vacation rentals and mandate affordable units in new construction [S3]
Emphasizes water conservation against data-center and energy-company demands, and opposes natural gas fracking threats to water [S2][S3]
Backs constitutional term limits and lowering Washington's voting age to 16 for state and local elections [S2]

taxes.basis: Supports a millionaire's tax and higher taxes on high-grossing businesses, with tax fairness as a platform plank.

spending.basis: Platform centers on expanded public investment: fully funding schools, raising teacher wages, and expanding healthcare access statewide.

housing.basis: Would regulate vacation rentals and mandate affordable units in new construction; regulatory rather than supply-side, but no social-housing or rent-stabilization agenda stated.

safety.basis: Emphasizes community policing and de-escalation rather than enforcement-first approaches.

climate.basis: Opposes natural gas fracking and prioritizes water/resource protection against energy-industry demands; no carbon-pricing position stated.

experience.basis: No prior elected government office; offers union bargaining leadership and lived-experience credentials rather than legislative tenure.

reform.basis: Supports constitutional term limits for elected offices and the federal judiciary plus lowering the voting age to 16 — structural-reform proposals no opponent raises.

healthcare.basis: Treats healthcare as a right and wants expanded access statewide, but does not explicitly call for single-payer.

tech.basis: Concerned that data centers' water and energy demands could crowd out community and agricultural needs.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 40
PDC contributions dataset (data.wa.gov kv7h-kjye), election_year 2026, filer "Margaret Catherine Carrido Adams (Maggie Adams)

contests.office_does: The single state senator for the 30th Legislative District (Federal Way, Algona, Pacific, Milton area), serving a four-year term voting on state budgets and legislation and confirming gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Claire Wilson, a 35-year educator with an early-learning-centered record, faces Republican first-time candidate Michael Rutland, a retired Air Force senior master sergeant and former Boeing manager. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Claire Wilson
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state senator and 35-year educator who served on the Federal Way School Board 2011-2019; her legislative focus is early learning, childcare, and youth services.
highlights: States she sponsored and passed "landmark investments" in affordable childcare and early learning, and secured over $7.5 million for the city of Pacific after flooding [S1]
Voted for law-enforcement pay raises and additional training alongside funding for drug treatment and recovery [S1]
Endorsed by firefighters, Planned Parenthood, teachers, nurses, the Alliance for Gun Responsibility, and the State Labor Council [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent senator with a legislative record in early learning and childcare plus eight years of prior school-board service.

spending.basis: Record of sponsoring and passing "landmark investments" in childcare and early learning and expanding services for young people.

housing.basis: Cites delivering resources for affordable housing serving seniors, low-income families, and people exiting homelessness — a public-investment approach.

safety.basis: Record spans both poles: voted for law-enforcement pay raises and training as well as funding for drug treatment and recovery.

social.basis: Sponsored medically accurate sexual-health education legislation; names reproductive rights a priority; endorsed by Planned Parenthood.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 41

candidates.name: Michael Rutland
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Federal Way, a retired U.S. Air Force Senior Master Sergeant with 24 years of service and four decades of management experience including at Boeing.
highlights: Opposes new income taxes and wants to repeal policies he says drive up fuel and grocery prices, calling for "responsible budgeting instead of higher taxes" [S1][S2]
Supports high academic standards, career and technical education, and parental involvement; says schools should not advance "political agendas" [S1][S2]
No endorsements listed in pamphlet statement or retrieved campaign-site content [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to new income taxes and calls for "responsible budgeting instead of higher taxes" across pamphlet and site.

spending.basis: Frames responsible budgeting, rather than new revenue or program expansion, as the answer to state costs.

climate.basis: Wants to repeal policies he says drive up fuel and grocery prices — an affordability-first posture, though he does not name the CCA.

social.basis: Emphasizes parental involvement and says schools should not advance "political agendas."

local-control.basis: Wants local leaders to have decision-making flexibility.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no legislative or elected record, running on military and Boeing management experience to bring "balance" to Olympia.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 41 (edition-2 page 95 is only the candidate index)

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 30th Legislative District (Federal Way and nearby south King County communities), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Jamila E. Taylor, chair of the House Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee, faces Republican first-time candidate Tiffany Bowyer, a small business owner running on affordability and spending accountability. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Jamila E. Taylor
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative seeking a third term, chair of the House Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee and an attorney with a legal-aid and victim-advocacy background.
highlights: Cites passing laws on gun-violence reduction, police training and trust, small-business tax reduction, and working-family tax credit expansion [S1]
Campaign highlights homeownership-access legislation and remediation of racially restrictive property deeds [S2]
Endorsed by the 30th District Democrats, State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, OneAmerica Votes, and Sierra Club; campaign site cites a Seattle Times endorsement [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Third-term incumbent and committee chair with a cited record of passed legislation; opponent is a first-time candidate.

safety.basis: Record emphasizes root causes of crime, gun-violence reduction, and police training/trust reform rather than enforcement-first measures.

taxes.basis: Record is targeted relief in both directions — small-business tax reductions and expanded working-family tax credits — with no stated position on new progressive revenue.

housing.basis: Emphasizes public resources for housing, a new homeownership program, and remediation of discriminatory deed covenants.

spending.basis: Platform leads with delivering resources for housing, childcare, and expanded tax credits — an investment orientation.

social.basis: Chairs the Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee and vice-chairs the Members of Color Caucus; endorsed by Planned Parenthood and OneAmerica Votes.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 42

candidates.name: Tiffany Bowyer
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Federal Way, a small business owner with a health-and-wellness background running on affordability, spending accountability, and police staffing.
highlights: Opposes new taxes on working families and businesses; says government spending growth is driving up property taxes, gas prices, and utility bills [S1][S2]
Calls for transparent tracking of taxpayer funds and program performance audits, citing untracked childcare funding and grant-oversight gaps [S2]
Proposes fully staffing police departments and holding repeat offenders accountable, coordinated with behavioral-health services [S2]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to new taxes on working families and businesses across pamphlet and campaign site.

spending.basis: Names spending growth as the driver of costs and calls specifically for transparent fund tracking and program performance audits.

safety.basis: Leads with fully staffing police and repeat-offender accountability, tempered by proposed coordination with behavioral-health services.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected experience or legislative record, running against an incumbent committee chair.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 42

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 30th Legislative District, serving a two-year term writing and voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Kristine Reeves, chair of the House Agriculture and Natural Resources Committee, faces Paul McDaniel, a former appointed Federal Way City Councilmember who prefers the Independent Party. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Kristine Reeves
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative (2017-2019 and 2023-present) and Federal Way small business owner who chairs the House Agriculture and Natural Resources Committee.
highlights: Says she passed laws protecting homeless students, expanding senior food access, mitigating wildfire risk, and investing $100 million in local public safety [S1]
Founded the bipartisan Veterans & Military Families Caucus and passed the Wounded Warrior Shared Leave Act [S1]
Endorsed by Congressman Adam Smith, labor, and environmental groups including Sierra Club Washington and Transportation for Washington [S1][S3][S4]

experience.basis: Multi-term incumbent with committee chairmanship and a cited record of passed legislation; opponent has no legislative experience.

spending.basis: Runs on program delivery: $100 million public-safety investment, senior food access, and protections for homeless students.

climate.basis: Cites wildfire-risk-mitigation legislation and holds Sierra Club Washington's 2026 primary endorsement.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 43

candidates.name: Paul McDaniel
candidates.summary: Former appointed Federal Way City Councilmember, Marine Corps Iraq War veteran, and technology-consulting business owner running with an Independent Party preference.
highlights: Says rising housing, food, gas, and energy costs are hurting working families and pledges to reduce taxes [S1]
Frames Olympia as marked by "fraud, corruption, and weak leadership" and stresses better management of existing government resources [S1][S2]
Would expand affordable housing by removing barriers to "middle housing," empower law enforcement, and "protect girls' sports" [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to reduce taxes, framing rising costs and "Olympia demanding more" as the problem.

spending.basis: Alleges fraud and weak leadership in Olympia and stresses managing existing resources rather than new spending.

housing.basis: Housing approach is removing barriers to "middle housing" — a supply-side mechanism — alongside a general affordable-housing goal.

safety.basis: Says he would empower law enforcement with necessary tools; no prevention-side proposals documented.

social.basis: Single stated plank to "protect girls' sports," a traditional-values position on gender policy.

experience.basis: Outsider change message against a multi-term incumbent, though he holds some elected experience via an appointed council seat.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 43

sources.outlet: City of Federal Way (council appointment announcement)

contests.office_does: The district's single member in the 49-seat Washington State Senate, elected to a four-year term to write and vote on state law and budgets and confirm gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Phil Fortunato, a senator since 2017 running on spending restraint and Second Amendment rights, faces Democrat Tamara Stramel, a first-time candidate and school-bond organizer. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Tamara Stramel
candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a precinct committee officer since 2017 and field organizer for the 2024 Sumner-Bonney Lake school bond, with a background in residential-treatment program management.
highlights: Says schools are "asked to do more with less" and pledges to champion education funding [S1]
Would advocate for the district to be prioritized in the state transportation budget and for rural infrastructure development [S1]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, AFL-CIO and Pierce County Democrats [S1]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with grassroots organizing background and no elected or legislative record, facing a legislator with roughly 13 years of service.

spending.basis: Argues schools and infrastructure are underfunded and pledges to champion education funding and transportation investment.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 44

candidates.name: Phil Fortunato
candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state senator since 2017 with prior House service, whose background is in environmental training and consulting and small contracting.
highlights: Attributes the state's financial challenges to "excessive spending and the unchecked growth of government programs" and pledges disciplined use of tax dollars [S1]
Pledges to "continue to stand firmly against efforts to erode our Second Amendment rights" [S1]
Campaign centers on resisting government overreach, safeguarding parental rights, and addressing crime and homelessness [S2]

experience.basis: Senator since 2017 with prior House terms (1998-2000, 2016) — roughly 13 years of cumulative legislative service.

spending.basis: Explicitly attributes state financial challenges to excessive spending and unchecked program growth; pledges disciplined use of tax dollars.

taxes.basis: Runs on tax restraint and disciplined use of tax dollars rather than new revenue, though no specific tax-cut proposals are documented.

safety.basis: Campaign addresses crime and homelessness; his pamphlet cites endorsements from unnamed law-enforcement leaders (self-reported).

social.basis: Campaign lists safeguarding parental rights among its central themes.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 44

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 31st Legislative District, serving a two-year term voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Drew Stokesbary, the sitting House Minority Leader, faces Democrat Stephen Szczurko-Walton, a first-time candidate, Navy veteran, and HVAC technician running on campaign-finance reform. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Drew Stokesbary
candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state representative from Auburn and the current House Minority Leader, an attorney advising startups and small businesses on corporate and securities law.
highlights: Selected House Republican leader in 2023; previously Ranking Member on House Appropriations and House Minority Floor Leader [S1][S3]
Says taxes "keep increasing, by billions each year" and called the 2026 session "an inflection point" given another budget shortfall [S1][S4]
Campaign site lists endorsements from the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs, Washington State PTA, and State Council of Fire Fighters [S2]

experience.basis: Sitting House Minority Leader with a long legislative tenure and prior Appropriations ranking-member and floor-leader roles.

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit critique of rising state taxes, including citing billions in new 2025 taxes and fees at the 2026 session opening.

safety.basis: Pledges "safer neighborhoods where laws are enforced and criminals are apprehended" — an enforcement-first framing with no prevention component.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 45

candidates.name: Stephen Szczurko-Walton
candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a U.S. Navy nuclear reactor operator from 2015-2024 and now a commercial HVAC technician, challenging the sitting House Minority Leader.
highlights: Signature issue is passing SB 6358 to "end corporate election spending in Washington," framed as a way to bypass Citizens United [S1][S2]
Pledges to "defend the Climate Commitment Act," modernize the electrical grid, and maintain diverse clean energy options [S1][S2]
Website advocates universal healthcare and progressive taxation to bring down housing, healthcare, and tax costs [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running a challenger campaign against the House Minority Leader.

reform.basis: Makes ending corporate election spending (SB 6358) his signature issue across pamphlet and website.

climate.basis: Explicitly pledges to defend the Climate Commitment Act and expand clean energy, repeated in both pamphlet and website.

healthcare.basis: Website advocates universal healthcare.

taxes.basis: Website advocates progressive taxation while pledging to bring down tax costs for regular households — a burden-shifting posture.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 45

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 31st Legislative District, serving a two-year term voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Joshua Penner, a first-term representative and former eight-year Orting mayor, faces Democrat John Bielka, a first-time candidate and career civil engineer/public works director. Both emphasize affordability and accountability; both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: John Bielka
candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a civil engineer and public works director with more than 30 years managing infrastructure, budgets, and operations, who raises cattle outside Orting.
highlights: Pledges to "work across the aisle to lower everyday costs" with responsible budgeting and reduced unnecessary regulation [S1][S2]
Emphasizes faster delivery of transportation, housing, and flood-protection projects and expanded career and technical education [S1][S2]
Website states support for the Second Amendment and for adequate police staffing; campaign tagline is "Results, not rhetoric" [S2]

experience.basis: Self-described "first time public servant" with deep professional but no elected or legislative experience.

spending.basis: Emphasizes responsible budgeting, "transparent math," bill-reading, and taxpayer protection — an accountability-first framing.

safety.basis: Supports adequate police staffing; no prevention-side proposals documented.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 46

candidates.name: Joshua Penner
candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican first-term state representative, an eight-year former mayor of Orting and Marine Corps Iraq War veteran serving as Assistant Ranking Member on Appropriations.
highlights: Pamphlet cites saving Buckley's Rainier School, preventing early-education cuts, and cutting red tape to fund White River and Fairfax bridge repairs [S1]
Led efforts to modify the "Keeping Families Together Act" and challenged Sound Transit-related legislation (SB 6309) on zoning bypass and financial-accountability grounds [S2]
Fourteen years of municipal elected service (mayor and councilmember) before the legislature [S1]

experience.basis: Fourteen years of municipal elected office plus one legislative term with cited accomplishments — a record profile, though short legislative tenure.

spending.basis: Brand is oversight and "real accountability" (agency oversight, Sound Transit financial scrutiny), though he also defended specific programs from cuts.

local-control.basis: Challenged Sound Transit-related legislation (SB 6309) on grounds it bypassed local zoning.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 46

contests.office_does: One of 49 members of the state Senate, elected to a four-year term to write state law, adopt state budgets, set tax policy, and confirm gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Two sitting Democratic legislators face each other: Rep. Cindy Ryu, in the House since 2011, is challenging two-term incumbent Sen. Jesse Salomon, calling him 'too moderate,' while Salomon calls himself a 'pragmatic progressive.' Republican first-time candidate Ira McBee runs on partisan balance in this heavily Democratic district.

candidates.name: Cindy Ryu
candidates.summary: Eight-term Democratic state representative and former Shoreline mayor, giving up her House seat to challenge the incumbent senator from her own party as insufficiently progressive.
highlights: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax; campaigns on a fairer tax system asking the wealthiest to pay their share [S1][S2][S6]
Housing approach leans on mixed-income and social housing plus streamlined permitting and modular construction [S5][S6]

Wants aggressive AI regulation and a comprehensive state privacy law; opposes data-center subsidies, though not the facilities themselves, while supporting AI and quantum development paired with regulation; passed a ban on AI deepfake likeness content [S4][S5][S6]
16 years in the House, chairing the Housing Committee for five years and now Technology, Economic Development & Veterans [S3][S6]
Holds the 32nd District Democrats' endorsement over the incumbent, plus the Washington State Labor Council and WEA [S1][S8]

taxes.basis: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax and campaigns on making the wealthiest pay their share.

spending.basis: Runs on fully funding public education and challenges the incumbent as 'too moderate,' positioning toward public investment over restraint.

housing.basis: Emphasizes mixed-income and social housing, though paired with permitting streamlining and modular construction.

climate.basis: Has backed climate initiatives and wants clean-air legislation targeting working-class communities and communities of color.

experience.basis: Longest legislative tenure in the race: in the House since 2011, five years chairing Housing, current committee chair, with bills signed into law.

tech.basis: Wants aggressive AI regulation and a comprehensive state privacy law, opposes data-center subsidies (though not the facilities themselves) while supporting regulated AI and quantum development, and passed a deepfake-likeness ban.

32nd District Democrats 2026 endorsements page

candidates.name: Ira McBee
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, entrepreneur, and international humanitarian worker from Lynnwood/Edmonds, running on partisan balance against one-party control.
highlights: Attributes rising costs to regulatory burdens and government spending rather than revenue shortfalls [S2][S3]
Supports 'responsible budgeting' and reforming spending inefficiency [S1][S3]
Backs rehabilitation for first-time offenders paired with strengthened penalties for repeat offenders [S2][S3]
Supports parental involvement in education, transparency, and expanded skilled-trades pathways [S1][S2]
Has raised under $8,000, roughly one-twentieth of either Democratic opponent [S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Frames affordability as a spending-and-regulation problem, not a revenue shortfall; no specific tax proposals in the dossier.

spending.basis: Runs on responsible budgeting and reforming spending inefficiency as core planks.

safety.basis: Wants to restore safety on transit and in public spaces with stronger repeat-offender penalties, softened by rehabilitation for first-time offenders.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no governing record, running explicitly as balance against entrenched one-party control.

social.basis: Emphasizes parental involvement and transparency in education, with a background in church ministry and homeschool-cooperative instruction.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 29
data.wa.gov PDC contributions dataset kv7h-kjye, filer_id MCBEI--940, election year 2026

candidates.name: Jesse Salomon
candidates.summary: Two-term incumbent Democratic senator, former public defender and child welfare prosecutor, who chairs the Senate Local Government Committee and self-describes as a 'pragmatic progressive.'
highlights: Supports a 9.9% 'Millionaires Tax' on incomes over $1 million; passed ESSB 5794 closing corporate tax loopholes and expanded low-income tax rebates [S1][S2][S5]
Emphasizes auditing homelessness spending rather than simply increasing budgets, and addressing the Aurora corridor 'with compassion and accountability' [S1][S4]
Passed ESSB 5611 cutting permitting red tape to speed housing, while his pamphlet also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI-powered rent-setting [S1][S2][S6]
Banned ICE face coverings by law and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state [S1][S2]
Endorsement base of sitting officeholders — legislators, mayors, the Snohomish County Sheriff [S1]

taxes.basis: Champions a 9.9% millionaires tax and has an enacted record of closing corporate tax loopholes while expanding low-income rebates.

spending.basis: Stresses auditing homelessness program spending over budget increases and pairs compassion with accountability, though he also backs major public investment packages.

housing.basis: Genuinely mixed: leads with supply expansion, permitting streamlining, and senior tax relief, but also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI rent-setting.

safety.basis: Mixed posture: calls for connecting people to mental health and addiction treatment, framed 'with compassion and accountability,' and carries the Snohomish County Sheriff's endorsement.

climate.basis: Supports a fracking ban, environmental restoration, and a transportation package his campaign calls among the state's greenest; no explicit carbon-pricing stance in the dossier.

experience.basis: Two-term incumbent with a committee chairmanship, prior JLARC chairmanship, seven years on the Shoreline council, and bills passed each session.

immigration.basis: Passed the ICE face-covering ban and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state against federal overreach.

tech.basis: Blocked AI-powered rent-setting and is working on algorithmic pricing regulation, but has no stated position on data-center subsidies.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 30

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat vacated by 16-year incumbent Cindy Ryu drew five Democrats — three sitting city councilmembers and two first-time candidates with professional-expertise pitches — plus one Republican, Lisa Rezac. With the Democratic vote split five ways in a top-two primary, the real contest is which Democrat advances, and the sharpest divide among them is tax approach.

candidates.name: Jenna Nand
candidates.summary: Edmonds City Councilmember since 2022 and business attorney, running on progressive revenue and affordability shaped by her caregiving and immigrant-family experience.
highlights: Supports repealing the 1% property tax cap, has floated a flat state income tax, and calls for 'progressive revenue' [S2][S3]
Supports affordable homeownership including state land trusts, and argues 'building more housing is not enough' without dedicated social-services funding [S1][S2][S3]
Pledges to protect Apple Health (Medicaid) and state health programs from federal threats [S2]
Says she is the only candidate endorsed by the teachers' union via WEA-PAC; also endorsed by Snohomish County Executive Dave Somers [S1][S2]
Backs climate resiliency measures, citing increasingly common extreme weather [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Supports repealing the 1% property tax cap, has floated an income tax, and campaigns on 'progressive revenue.'

spending.basis: Consistent investment platform: dedicated social-services funding for homelessness, statewide school funding, and subsidized college costs.

housing.basis: Backs state land trusts for affordable homeownership and says building more housing is not enough without social services.

climate.basis: Supports climate resiliency measures, citing extreme weather; no carbon-pricing position in the dossier.

experience.basis: Sitting councilmember since 2022 (appointed, then elected; Council President Pro Tem), a shorter governing record than the field's most tenured.

immigration.basis: Pro bono work with the Northwest Immigrant Rights Project; no stated enforcement-policy position.

healthcare.basis: Pledges to protect Apple Health/Medicaid within existing structures from federal threats; no system-transformation agenda stated.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 31
WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye, 2026, LD32 State Representative)

candidates.name: Will Chen
candidates.summary: Two-term Edmonds City Councilmember, licensed CPA, and former Fortune 500 auditor who chaired the council's Finance Committee and leads with fiscal-management credentials.
highlights: Wants to remove the 1% property tax cap so revenue can match inflation, while describing himself as a social liberal who is financially 'careful' [S4]
Brought cost-reduction proposals during Edmonds' 2024 budget difficulties, including reduced city hall hours and police-leadership restructuring [S6]
Supports housing and homelessness solutions combining affordable housing with wraparound services [S3]
Re-elected to the Edmonds council in 2025 with 88.3% of the vote [S1][S6]
Endorsements come mostly from local mayors and councilmembers — plus former U.S. Rep. Brian Baird — rather than state legislators, labor, or newspapers [S2][S3]

taxes.basis: Supports lifting the 1% property tax cap so revenue tracks inflation, framed cautiously rather than as broad progressive-revenue expansion.

spending.basis: CPA/auditor profile, self-described financial carefulness, and a council record of concrete cost-reduction proposals during a budget crunch.

housing.basis: Backs affordable housing paired with wraparound services — a public-investment framing rather than deregulation.

experience.basis: Two-term sitting councilmember and finance chair with a governance record, though no legislative tenure.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 31
WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye, 2026, LD32 State Representative)

candidates.name: Keith Scully
candidates.summary: Shoreline City Councilmember since 2016 and former mayor, with a career spanning paramedic, King County deputy prosecutor, UN war crimes prosecutor, and environmental-nonprofit legal director.
highlights: Supports restructuring the state tax code to mirror the federal progressive system and taxing business profit rather than revenue [S2][S3][S4]
Proposes addressing the state budget deficit with across-the-board cuts sparing education [S3]
Supports expanding affordable housing, including upzoning near transit; cites a Shoreline record on housing, transit, and fielding mental health professionals [S1][S2][S3]
Campaigns on fighting climate change through electrification of fleets, homes, and businesses; endorsed by Sierra Club Washington [S2][S3][S6]
The only candidate endorsed by both the Seattle Times and The Stranger editorial boards, plus retiring Rep. Cindy Ryu [S1][S2][S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly proposes progressive restructuring of the state tax code, including taxing business profit rather than revenue.

spending.basis: Proposes across-the-board budget cuts except education to address the deficit — an explicit restraint plank unusual in this Democratic field.

housing.basis: Mixed approach: upzoning near transit (supply-side) alongside expanding affordable housing investment in his Shoreline record.

safety.basis: Cites bringing mental health professionals into the field in Shoreline; a services-alongside-response record rather than enforcement-first.

climate.basis: Campaigns on fighting climate change via broad electrification and carries the Sierra Club Washington endorsement.

experience.basis: Longest local-government tenure in the field: planning commission since 2010, council since 2016, deputy mayor and mayor, endorsed by the retiring incumbent.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 32
WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye, 2026, LD32 State Representative)
Sierra Club Washington 2026 primary endorsements

candidates.name: Danica Noble
candidates.summary: First-time candidate and attorney with roughly 20 years at the Federal Trade Commission, most recently on the antitrust case against Meta; the race's top fundraiser.
highlights: Core platform is challenging monopoly power and corporate consolidation in tech, healthcare, and groceries, framed as lowering consumer prices [S1][S2]
Proposes tech guardrails for children and workers: ed-tech data limits, regulating AI in classrooms, banning school cellphones, and preventing surveillance pricing [S1][S2][S3]
Prioritizes fully funding public schools and moving away from per-student head-count funding [S2][S3][S4]
Advocates budget transparency and examining state liability in lawsuit settlements as a cost-cutting measure [S4]
Top fundraiser by a wide margin, with the Washington State Labor Council and a bloc of legislators behind her [S1][S2][S5]

spending.basis: Mixed: pairs fully funding public schools with budget transparency and settlement-liability review as cost-cutting.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, though her pitch is federal enforcement expertise rather than outsider renewal.

tech.basis: Tech/AI regulation is her core platform — ed-tech data limits, classroom AI rules, surveillance-pricing bans — backed by FTC antitrust litigation experience.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 32
WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye, 2026, LD32 State Representative)

candidates.name: Lisa Rezac
candidates.summary: The race's only Republican, a fundraising auctioneer and former LD32/King County GOP officer who lost to Rep. Ryu in 2024 with about 31% and runs against one-party control.
highlights: Campaign slogan 'Less Waste. Lower Taxes. Better Results.'; opposes new taxes and fee increases [S2][S3]
Pledges to expose 'waste, fraud, and mismanagement,' with independent audits of major state programs [S1][S2][S3]
Campaigns on parental rights in education and 'restoring excellence' [S1][S3]
Prioritizes safer communities and support for law enforcement [S1][S3]
Raised $4,058, the least in the race, and lists no endorsements [S1][S2][S4]

taxes.basis: Opposes new taxes and fee increases and advocates tax relief as her campaign slogan's centerpiece.

spending.basis: Platform is organized around exposing waste and fraud, independent audits of major state programs, and zero tolerance for misuse of funds.

safety.basis: Prioritizes safer communities and support for law enforcement; no specific enforcement proposals in the dossier.

experience.basis: No elective office record; runs explicitly against one-party control, though she is a longtime party organizer and repeat candidate.

social.basis: Campaigns on parental rights in education; no further social-policy specifics in the dossier.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 33

candidates.name: Chris Bloomquist
candidates.summary: First-time candidate, recruiting executive, and founder of a climate-focused recruiting company, recruited to run by Rep. Lauren Davis and endorsed by both sitting LD32 legislators.
highlights: Supports the state's millionaires tax but opposes raising taxes on working families [S4]
Campaigns on growing Washington's green economy through clean energy investment and solar/wind development [S2][S3]
Prioritizes fully funding public schools and detaching school funding from student head counts [S3][S4]
Testified in support of legislation to 'demask ICE' and champions immigrant communities [S1]
Endorsed by Sen. Salomon, Rep. Davis, and the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs — a law-enforcement endorsement uncommon in this Democratic field [S1][S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Supports keeping the millionaires tax while opposing new taxes on working families — pro-progressive-revenue but not expansionist.

spending.basis: Mixed: leads with fully funding public schools while pledging to scrutinize spending and expect results.

safety.basis: Endorsed by the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs and leads first-responder roundtables on Aurora Avenue safety, while pledging evidence-based homelessness approaches.

climate.basis: Founded a climate-focused recruiting company and campaigns centrally on clean-energy investment and green-economy growth.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elective record, though recruited and backed by the district's sitting legislators.

immigration.basis: Testified in support of 'demask ICE' legislation and campaigns on support for immigrant communities.

social.basis: States 'women's rights are paramount' and champions civil rights.

tech.basis: Pledges to author AI legislation protecting children.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 32nd Legislative District (Shoreline, Lynnwood, Mountlake Terrace, Edmonds area), serving a two-year term voting on state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: An intra-Democratic contest: incumbent Lauren Davis, known for her behavioral-health legislative record, is challenged by Imraan Siddiqi, Executive Director of CAIR-Washington, who runs with a progressive-movement coalition. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.name: Lauren Davis
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative since 2019 whose career centers on behavioral health, including founding the Washington Recovery Alliance and championing Ricky's Law.
highlights: Passed Ricky's Law (involuntary crisis commitment for life-threatening addiction) and created the Recovery Navigator Program, plus treatment programs inside jails and ERs [S1][S2][S3]
Has "championed healthcare for immigrants and protections against ICE" and describes herself as "completely pro-choice" [S1]
Endorsed by Governors Ferguson and Gregoire, Representatives DelBene, Larsen, and Smith, the State Labor Council, and a broad union slate [S1]

experience.basis: Seven-year incumbent with one of the Legislature's most identifiable single-issue records (Ricky's Law, Recovery Navigator Program).

safety.basis: Treatment-and-recovery-led record (Recovery Navigator, jail/ER treatment, crisis care center), tempered by the involuntary-commitment mechanism of Ricky's Law.

immigration.basis: States she has championed healthcare for immigrants and protections against ICE.

social.basis: Describes herself as "completely pro-choice."

spending.basis: Record is built on funding and expanding programs: treatment access, domestic-violence victim services, and the Lynnwood Crisis Care Center.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 34

candidates.name: Imraan Siddiqi
candidates.summary: First-time Democratic candidate and Executive Director of CAIR-Washington, a civil rights advocate and former small business owner challenging the incumbent with a progressive-movement coalition.
highlights: Core planks are protecting immigrant communities facing "federal targeting" and building "a Washington that stands up to federal overreach" [S1][S2]
One of the original plaintiffs against the Trump administration's "Muslim Ban"; organizes coalitions with labor unions and tenant-rights advocates [S1]
Reported endorsements include U.S. Reps. Rashida Tlaib and Ilhan Omar, 32nd LD seatmate Rep. Cindy Ryu, UFCW 3000 (dual), and the Transit Riders Union [S3]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, explicitly framing the race as current leaders not doing enough.

immigration.basis: CAIR-Washington executive director, Muslim Ban plaintiff, and a platform centered on protecting immigrants from federal targeting.

housing.basis: Pledges to fight for housing affordability and organizes coalitions with tenant-rights advocates.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 34

contests.office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, elected to a four-year term to write and vote on state law and budgets covering tax, housing, healthcare, education, and transportation policy.
contests.race_blurb: Only one candidate filed for this South King County seat around Sea-Tac Airport: appointed incumbent Tina L. Orwall (D), who advances from the primary unopposed. She was appointed in December 2024 to succeed longtime Senator Karen Keiser after 15+ years in the State House.

candidates.name: Tina L. Orwall
candidates.summary: Appointed incumbent State Senator and career social worker, serving the 33rd District in the Legislature since 2009, first in the House (including as Speaker Pro Tempore) and in the Senate since December 2024.
highlights: Appointed to the Senate in December 2024 after roughly 15 years as a 33rd District state representative, including service as Speaker Pro Tempore [S1][S3]

Career social worker with twenty years in behavioral health; Vice Chair of the Senate Health & Long-Term Care Committee, with priorities including the 988 crisis line and behavioral-health capacity [S1]
Cites bills prohibiting insurers from using AI to deny care and a 2024 law against non-consensual deepfake intimate images, plus airport pollution mitigation for Sea-Tac communities [S1][S2]

spending.basis: Platform centers on investing in behavioral-health capacity, flood recovery, and backstopping programs threatened by federal cuts.

safety.basis: Behavioral-health career and priorities (988 crisis line, behavioral-health capacity, protecting vulnerable populations) emphasize prevention and services.

experience.basis: In the Legislature since 2009 with House leadership (Speaker Pro Tempore) and Senate committee vice-chairmanship — a long enacted record.

healthcare.basis: Healthcare work operates within the existing system — insurer regulation (AI denials), 988 crisis line, and behavioral-health capacity — with no single-payer platform.

tech.basis: Enacted record of AI and tech regulation: prohibiting insurers from using AI to deny care, protections against 'unregulated AI,' and the 2024 deepfake intimate-images law.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 35 (same statement at edition-2 page 47)

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Edwin Obras, who narrowly won a 2025 special election, faces two first-time Republican challengers — Chris Martinez, who has the King County GOP endorsement and a detailed conservative platform, and Darryl K. Jones, who runs a one-theme fiscal-restraint message. The primary effectively decides which Republican joins Obras on the November ballot.

candidates.name: Edwin Obras
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat and Assistant Majority Whip, a Filipino immigrant and 18-year Seattle Human Services Department veteran who won the seat by 641 votes in the 2025 special election.
highlights: Champions rent-increase caps and anti-displacement housing legislation, framing affordability around rising rents [S1][S2]
Six bills signed into law in his first full session, including isolated-worker protections, strengthened firearm background checks, and rideshare regulation [S1][S2]
Pledges to continue 'fighting for immigrant rights' [S1]
Says he secured over $30 million in state investments for the district, including housing and health projects [S1]
Backed by the Washington State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, Washington Conservation Action, and Gov. Ferguson [S1]

spending.basis: Frames state investment as the affordability answer — $30 million secured for district projects and expanded mental health resources — with no restraint planks.

housing.basis: Champions rent-increase caps and anti-displacement legislation as his core housing agenda.

safety.basis: Focuses on jail reform and community safety standards, alongside background-check and police-impersonation laws.

climate.basis: Endorsed by Washington Conservation Action; no stated climate-policy position in the dossier.

experience.basis: Incumbent with six bills signed in his first full session, an Assistant Majority Whip post, and 18 years in city human-services leadership.

immigration.basis: Pledges in his pamphlet statement to continue 'fighting for immigrant rights.'

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood; no detailed social-policy statements in the dossier.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 36 (also edition-2 page 48)

candidates.name: Darryl K. Jones
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Kent with a career in customer-facing healthcare and service roles, running a single-theme fiscal-restraint message.
highlights: Core message: 'Washington has a spending problem'; pledges never to vote to increase constituents' costs [S1]
Runs on 'policy over party,' arguing one-party dominance has not benefited voters [S1]
No endorsements, itemized fundraising, or policy positions beyond the pamphlet statement were found [S1][S2][S3]

taxes.basis: Criticizes Democratic majorities for higher taxes and pledges never to vote to increase constituents' costs.

spending.basis: His core message is that Washington has a spending problem requiring restraint.

experience.basis: First-time candidate whose only political role is precinct committee officer, running against one-party dominance.

candidates.name: Chris Martinez
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate and blue-collar tradesman with the King County GOP endorsement, running the most detailed conservative platform in the race.
highlights: Would work to eliminate the Climate Commitment Act, which he blames for high energy costs, and pledges to 'abolish energy-killing taxes' [S1][S2]
Wants lower taxes — especially B&O taxes — and reduced spending and regulation [S2]
Supports increased law-enforcement presence and holding criminals accountable [S1][S2]
Opposes 'woke ideologies' in schools without parental consent; runs on parental rights and 'traditional family values' [S1][S2]
Names specific bills he opposes: HB 2165, SB 5925, and HB 2320, which he calls constitutional violations, plus SB 5974 concerning elected sheriffs [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to lower taxes, especially B&O taxes, and to 'abolish energy-killing taxes.'

spending.basis: Wants to reduce wasteful spending and regulation as a core plank.

safety.basis: Supports increased law-enforcement presence and holding criminals accountable.

climate.basis: Explicitly targets the Climate Commitment Act for elimination, blaming it for high energy costs.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record who calls for replacing career politicians with limited-government leaders.

social.basis: Runs on parental rights, 'traditional family values,' religious liberty, and opposition to 'woke ideologies' in schools.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 37 (also edition-2 page 49)

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Twelve-year Democratic incumbent Mia Gregerson, Vice-Chair of House Appropriations, faces a same-party challenge from Burien City Councilmember Alex Andrade, who stresses spending accountability, plus Republican first-time candidate Yuri Marinchik running an anti-career-politician campaign.

candidates.name: Mia Su-Ling Gregerson
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat since 2013, former SeaTac mayor, and Vice-Chair of House Appropriations who runs on delivered programs like free school meals and the Right to Repair law.
highlights: Sponsored HB 1483 (2025), Washington's Right to Repair Act, and HB 1404 requiring free school breakfast and lunch statewide from 2026-27 [S3][S4]
Points to state programs as the affordability answer: school meals, emergency food assistance, and free transit for kids [S1]
Vice-Chair of House Appropriations and former SeaTac mayor when the city passed the nation's first $15 minimum wage [S1]
Official legislative page highlights 2025-26 bills described as strengthening community safety and protecting immigrants [S2]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, OneAmerica Votes, Sierra Club Washington, and Gov. Ferguson [S1][S6]

spending.basis: Runs on expanding and delivering state programs — universal free school meals, food assistance, free youth transit, corridor funding — as the primary affordability lever.

climate.basis: Endorsed by Sierra Club Washington in this primary; no specific climate-policy statements in the dossier.

experience.basis: Representative since 2013 with a budget-writing leadership post, a sponsored-bill record, and prior service as SeaTac mayor.

immigration.basis: Official page highlights bills protecting immigrants, and she carries the OneAmerica Votes endorsement.

social.basis: Says she helped establish Washington's first Office of Equity.

tech.basis: Sponsored and campaigns on the Right to Repair law restricting manufacturers' parts-pairing practices — consumer-side tech regulation.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 38 (same statement at edition-2 page 50)

candidates.name: Yuri Marinchik
candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, a working-class immigrant building a small business with his wife, running against 'career politicians' on cost of living.
highlights: Says he will fight for lower housing and energy costs and 'fair taxes' [S1]
Criticizes 'endless rules that make life harder and increase government control' [S1]
Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge supporting a congressional term-limits convention [S4]
Minimal fundraising (~$1,500) and no endorsements found beyond the term-limits organization [S3][S4]

spending.basis: Frames government rules and control as the problem itself; a restraint orientation without specific budget proposals.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected or governmental record, running explicitly against career politicians.

reform.basis: Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge — a documented structural-reform commitment.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 38 (same statement at edition-2 page 50)

candidates.name: Alex Andrade
candidates.summary: Burien City Councilmember elected in 2024 and small business owner, challenging the 12-year Democratic incumbent on spending accountability and public safety.
highlights: Says state spending 'continues to grow without enough accountability' and pledges transparency in how taxpayer dollars are spent [S1]
Supports co-response teams pairing first responders with behavioral health professionals, and 'responsible policies to address large unmanaged encampments' [S2]
Opposes unfunded state mandates on cities, arguing cities should make decisions reflecting their unique needs [S2]
Supports pro-choice protections and equality [S1]
Endorsement base is South King County city officials rather than state figures, labor, or party organizations [S2]

spending.basis: Central plank is that state spending grows without enough accountability, demanding transparency — an accountability lane, not an anti-spending one.

safety.basis: Mixed: backs behavioral-health co-response teams while calling for accountability-focused policies on large unmanaged encampments.

experience.basis: First-term councilmember (2024) and small business owner running on local-government freshness against a 12-year incumbent.

local-control.basis: Explicitly opposes unfunded state mandates on cities and argues for city-level decision-making.

social.basis: Pamphlet lists support for pro-choice protections and equality.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 39 (same statement at edition-2 page 51)

contests.office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, writing state law and budgets in Olympia for the 34th District (West Seattle, Vashon Island, White Center, west Burien).
contests.race_blurb: Appointed incumbent Emily Alvarado (D) is the only candidate filed for this seat, opened when Sen. Joe Nguyen resigned in January 2025 to lead the state Department of Commerce. She advances to the general election unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.name: Emily Alvarado
candidates.summary: Sitting State Senator appointed in January 2025 after two years as the district's State Representative; an attorney and affordable-housing executive who previously directed the Seattle Office of Housing.
highlights: Former Director of the Seattle Office of Housing, where she says she oversaw $290 million in anti-displacement, supportive housing, renter protection, and homeownership investments [S1]
Says she championed the 2026 'millionaires tax' paired with tax relief for working families and small businesses [S1]
Cites protections passed for renters, workers, and consumers, including housing-investor caps and a surge-pricing regulation bill (SB 5600) [S1][S3]

taxes.basis: Says she championed the 2026 'millionaires tax' as historic tax reform, paired with relief for working families — an enacted progressive-revenue record.

spending.basis: Platform emphasizes public investment: school funding, a stronger safety net for youth and seniors, and affordability programs.

housing.basis: Career record of public housing investment and renter protection (Seattle Office of Housing, $290M in programs) plus legislative work on housing-investor caps.

climate.basis: Pamphlet states support for climate action alongside reproductive freedom and gun reform; no detail on carbon-pricing specifics.

experience.basis: Sitting (appointed) senator with prior House service (2023-2025) and executive-branch housing leadership — a record-based profile, though elected tenure is short.

social.basis: Pamphlet states support for reproductive freedom and 'common sense gun reform.'

sources.ref: edition-1 page 40
edition-2 page 52

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 34th District (West Seattle, Vashon Island, White Center, west Burien) in the 98-member Washington House, serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Brianna K. Thomas (D), appointed in January 2025 to fill Emily Alvarado's House seat and confirmed in the November 2025 special election, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.name: Brianna K. Thomas
candidates.summary: First-term incumbent State Representative, appointed in January 2025 and confirmed by voters that November; a labor organizer and former Seattle City Hall chief of staff with a democracy-reform background.
highlights: Managed the 2013 SeaTac $15 minimum wage campaign and led Seattle's Honest Elections initiative that created the democracy voucher program [S1][S3]
Says she passed bills guaranteeing meal and rest breaks for healthcare workers and protections for domestic workers, and contributed to passing the 'Millionaires Tax' [S1]
Former Chief of Staff to Seattle City Council President Lorena González and Mayor's Office labor liaison; now a Senior Policy Advisor at Seattle's Human Services Department [S1][S4]

taxes.basis: Says she contributed to passing the 'Millionaires Tax,' describing it as tax reform benefiting working Washingtonians.

housing.basis: Former field director for the Washington Low Income Housing Alliance Action Fund; affordable housing is a recurring theme of her organizing career.

experience.basis: First-term legislator, but with a long insider career (City Hall chief of staff, mayoral labor liaison) and bills passed — a record-leaning profile rather than an outsider one.

reform.basis: Led Seattle's Honest Elections initiative creating the democracy voucher program — a concrete structural campaign-finance reform record.

social.basis: Says she passed legislation ensuring employees' privacy and bodily autonomy.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 40
edition-2 page 52

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 34th District (West Seattle, Vashon Island, White Center, west Burien), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Fifteen-year incumbent and House Majority Leader Joe Fitzgibbon faces political newcomer Mary Anito; both prefer the Democratic Party and both advance to the general. The visible contrasts are experience (legislative leadership vs. private-sector operations) and fiscal emphasis, with a notable evidence asymmetry since Anito's platform detail is limited.

candidates.name: Joe Fitzgibbon
candidates.summary: State Representative since 2010 and House Majority Leader since 2022, with nine years chairing the House Environment & Energy Committee and a signature record on climate legislation and the 2026 tax-reform package.
highlights: Championed the Climate Commitment Act and Clean Fuel Standard; pamphlet describes passing 'the nation's strongest climate laws' [S1][S3]
Says he led passage of the 'millionaires' tax' paired with tax cuts for working families and small businesses [S1]
House Majority Leader since 2022 after chairing Environment & Energy from 2014 to 2022; now also on Appropriations and Rules [S1][S3]

taxes.basis: Says he led passage of the millionaires-tax reform package — an enacted progressive-revenue record.

spending.basis: Pamphlet emphasizes securing investments in education, healthcare, transit, public safety, and the environment, alongside budget stabilization.

housing.basis: Supports both supply-side measures (building near transit, modernizing local planning) and public-investment measures (affordable housing, homelessness prevention, limiting displacement).

climate.basis: Championed the Climate Commitment Act and Clean Fuel Standard and pledges to continue advancing clean energy and emission reductions.

experience.basis: Fifteen-year incumbent and House Majority Leader — among the most senior legislators on the King County ballot.

local-control.basis: Supports 'modernizing local planning' and served on the board of Futurewise, a growth-management advocacy organization — an indirect lean toward state/regional standards.

social.basis: Defends reproductive freedom, voting rights, and racial and environmental justice in both pamphlet and campaign materials.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 41
edition-2 page 53

candidates.name: Mary Anito
candidates.summary: First-time candidate with an operations and supply-chain management background at REI, Wayfair, Caterpillar, and Cisco, running on pairing fiscal responsibility with public investment.
highlights: Argues 'fiscal responsibility and strong public investment are not mutually exclusive'; wants to balance the budget while funding infrastructure, education, and healthcare [S1]
Operations and engineering background (MIT Leaders for Global Operations Fellow; senior operations manager at REI) with board service at ROOTS Young Adult Shelter [S1]
Political newcomer challenging the House Majority Leader; platform detail beyond the pamphlet was not retrievable [S1][S4]

spending.basis: Explicitly pairs balancing the budget with funding infrastructure, education, and healthcare — a stated mix of restraint and investment.

experience.basis: First-time candidate and political newcomer running on private-sector operations experience rather than any governmental record.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 41
edition-2 page 53
