contests.office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, serving four-year terms writing state law and budgets for the 36th District (Ballard, Magnolia, Queen Anne, Greenwood area of northwest Seattle).
contests.race_blurb: The clearest partisan contrast among the light races: incumbent Democrat Noel Frame, the Legislature's leading progressive-tax architect, versus Republican party officer Jillian England, whose platform is built on repealing the Millionaires Tax and the Climate Commitment Act. Both advance to the general in this two-candidate race.

candidates.name: Noel C. Frame
candidates.summary: Incumbent State Senator since 2023 after seven years as the district's State Representative; Vice Chair of Finance on Senate Ways & Means and the Legislature's most prominent advocate for progressive tax restructuring.
highlights: Prime sponsor of Washington's proposed wealth tax since 2021 (SB 5486, SB 5797) and helped pass the capital gains tax and the 2026 'Millionaires Tax' [S1][S3][S4]
Cites expansion of the Working Families Tax Credit to 460,000 new households, a senior property tax exemption, and low-income energy assistance [S1]
Ten years in the Legislature, with finance-committee leadership in both chambers (past House Finance chair, current Ways & Means Vice Chair of Finance) [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Prime sponsor of the wealth tax since 2021 and helped pass the capital gains tax and Millionaires Tax; calls the tax code 'upside-down' and regressive.

spending.basis: Platform is built on raising revenue to fund schools, healthcare, human services, and early learning, with expanded credit and assistance programs.

experience.basis: Ten-year incumbent legislator with committee leadership in both chambers and a signature enacted tax record.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 42

candidates.name: Jillian England
candidates.summary: Republican challenger and longtime party precinct officer with a career in computer science and healthcare business operations, running on repealing the Millionaires Tax and the Climate Commitment Act.
highlights: Top priority is repealing the 9.9% 'Millionaires Tax,' which she characterizes as a door to broader income taxation; also targets the sales tax and B&O tax [S1][S2]
Would repeal the Climate Commitment Act, citing 50-65 cents per gallon in gas costs, and supports an 'all-of-the-above' energy strategy including nuclear and natural gas [S1][S2]
Calls for accountability on homelessness, clearing encampments, and 'breaking feedback loops' between addiction, migration, and policy tolerance [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Platform centers on repealing the Millionaires Tax and reducing the sales tax and B&O tax.

spending.basis: Repeated emphasis on accountability, cost-efficiency, and deregulation; states 'government force cannot create prosperity, safety, or abundance.'

safety.basis: Calls for clearing encampments, accountability, and breaking addiction-policy 'feedback loops' — an enforcement-and-accountability approach to street disorder.

climate.basis: Would repeal the Climate Commitment Act on gas-price grounds and expand nuclear, natural gas, and hydroelectric energy.

experience.basis: No prior elected office beyond party precinct officer; runs on private-sector cost-efficiency experience.

social.basis: Says schools should 'educate students rather than indoctrinate them' and demands accountability — parental-rights-inflected framing on curricula.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 42

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 36th District (Ballard, Magnolia, Queen Anne, Greenwood area of northwest Seattle), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Julia Grant Reed, first elected in 2022, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.name: Julia Grant Reed
candidates.summary: Second-term incumbent State Representative and workforce-development consultant with prior policy experience in the Obama administration and Seattle city government.
highlights: Distinguishing focus is workforce development: investing in apprenticeship pathways, youth employment, and higher education, drawn from her consulting career [S1]
Prior service as Senior Policy Advisor & Regional Affairs Director for the City of Seattle and in the Obama administration at the State Department and OMB [S1]
Pamphlet endorsements include the State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates, Alliance for Gun Responsibility, and WA Nurses Association PAC [S1]

climate.basis: Pledges to be a strong voice for climate-friendly transportation solutions.

experience.basis: Second-term incumbent with federal (Obama administration) and City of Seattle policy experience — a record-based profile with moderate tenure.

social.basis: Endorsement pattern includes Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates and Alliance for Gun Responsibility.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 43

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 36th District (Ballard, Magnolia, Queen Anne, Greenwood area of northwest Seattle), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Liz Berry, House Labor Committee chair and prime sponsor of the 2025 permit-to-purchase firearm law, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.name: Liz Berry
candidates.summary: Third-term incumbent State Representative serving since 2021 and chair of the House Labor Committee, with a signature record on gun-safety legislation and a career in reproductive-rights and consumer advocacy.
highlights: Prime sponsor of HB 1163 (2025), Washington's permit-to-purchase firearm law, signed by Gov. Ferguson in May 2025 [S3]
Chairs the House Labor Committee; cites legislation protecting workers from wage theft and unfair noncompete agreements [S1]
Former Legislative Director for Congresswoman Gabby Giffords and past Pro-Choice Washington board member; has worked to protect and expand abortion access [S1]

taxes.basis: Supported tax cuts on essentials while 'making our tax system fairer' to fund services, and calls the 2026 tax-code rebalancing package 'a historic step.'

experience.basis: Third-term incumbent with committee chairmanship and a concrete legislative signature (HB 1163), though tenure is moderate.

social.basis: Lifelong reproductive-justice advocate (past Pro-Choice Washington board) with legislative work to protect and expand abortion access, plus signature gun-safety law.

spending.basis: Pamphlet explicitly frames a fairer tax system as the means 'to fund schools, childcare, housing, and health care' — the same invest-and-expand plank that earned +1 spending scores for comparable candidates (Orwall, Alvarado, Fitzgibbon) in this batch. Omission is inconsistent.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 43

contests.office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, voting on state law, the state budget, and taxation for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton).
contests.race_blurb: An open seat after Sen. Rebecca Saldaña declined to seek reelection. Sitting State Rep. Chipalo Street, backed by Saldaña and much of the Democratic establishment, faces first-time candidate and environmental-justice organizer Tatiana Brown; both prefer the Democratic Party, overlap broadly on policy, and advance to the general.

candidates.name: Chipalo Street
candidates.summary: State Representative for the 37th District since 2023 and Microsoft technologist, running for the open Senate seat with the outgoing incumbent's endorsement and a record of bills passed and district funding secured.
highlights: Says he secured $131 million in state investment for the district, including Garfield Super Block, Skyway homeownership, and a reparations study [S1]
Championed the 'millionaires tax' and cites passage of rent stabilization, tenant protections, and zoning reform to increase home building [S1][S2]
Cites work restoring abortion funding, maintaining food assistance for immigrants, banning ICE mask-wearing, and stopping Flock camera surveillance [S1]

taxes.basis: Championed the millionaires tax to fund education and healthcare access; wants the wealthy to pay more.

spending.basis: Record emphasizes securing state investment ($131 million for district projects) as the lever for outcomes.

housing.basis: Passed tenant protections including rent stabilization, while also reforming zoning to increase home building — tilts to protections with a supply component.

experience.basis: Sitting legislator since 2023 with Democratic leadership role, passed bills, and establishment backing — the record candidate in this race, though tenure is short.

immigration.basis: Cites work maintaining food assistance for immigrants, banning ICE mask-wearing, and stopping Flock camera surveillance.

social.basis: Cites legislative work restoring abortion funding.

tech.basis: Cites stopping Flock camera surveillance — a surveillance-limiting posture — while working in the tech industry.

reform.basis: Dossier documents the work-the-system profile explicitly: backing from the outgoing incumbent, House Speaker, AG, County Executive, and city councilmembers [S2], pamphlet-touted 'unmatched endorsements' [S1], and delivery framed through establishment coalition work. His opponent scored +2 on this axis from the same dossier pair; the contrast evidence for -1 is at least as strong as several medium scores elsewhere in this batch.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 44

candidates.name: Tatiana Brown
candidates.summary: First-time candidate and community organizer; co-chair of the Washington Environmental Justice Council, running a small-dollar campaign that rejects corporate and PAC money and emphasizes participatory governance.
highlights: Co-chairs the Washington Environmental Justice Council implementing the HEAL Act, and helped shape advocacy around the Climate Commitment Act [S1][S2]
Rejects corporate donations, PAC, fossil fuel, and defense contractor money; wants government-funded community assemblies in policymaking [S1][S2]
Supports progressive revenue so 'the wealthiest pay their fair share,' plus education funding, public health, and alternative public safety approaches [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Platform repeatedly calls for progressive revenue so 'the wealthiest pay their fair share.'

spending.basis: Priorities center on funding K-12, higher education, public health, and transit as levers for outcomes.

safety.basis: Lists 'alternative public safety approaches' among her public-service priorities.

climate.basis: Climate-justice platform grounded in a record: co-chairs the Environmental Justice Council implementing the HEAL Act and helped shape CCA advocacy.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with an organizer profile and no elected experience, contrasting with a sitting legislator.

reform.basis: Explicitly refuses corporate, PAC, fossil fuel, and defense contractor money; small-dollar funded and emphasizes participatory community assemblies.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 44

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton) in the 98-member Washington House, voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Sharon Tomiko Santos, in office since 1999 and chair of the House Education Committee, faces first-time candidate Kelabe Tewolde, who is running on generational change. Both prefer the Democratic Party, overlap broadly on policy, and advance to the general; the practical contrast is seniority versus new leadership.

candidates.name: Sharon Tomiko Santos
candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative since 1999 and chair of the House Education Committee; the first Japanese American woman elected to a mainland state legislature, with deep district and nonprofit roots.
highlights: Roughly 27 years of incumbency and the House Education Committee chairmanship — the deepest institutional seniority in this race [S1][S3]
As Education Chair, focused on accessible quality education, a 'fundamental reimagining' of education delivery, childcare affordability, and workforce pathways [S1][S2]
Deep district roots: Franklin High School alumna, founder of three nonprofits, founding board member of OneAmerica [S1]

taxes.basis: Pamphlet cites 'tax fairness for working families' without further specifics.

housing.basis: Supports both increasing housing supply and expanding affordable housing near transit while preventing displacement — a mixed supply-plus-protections posture.

safety.basis: Campaign emphasizes community-based violence prevention and expanded behavioral health services.

experience.basis: Serving since 1999 with a committee chairmanship — a maximal seniority and institutional-record profile.

immigration.basis: Founding board member of OneAmerica (Hate Free Zone), an immigrant-advocacy organization — an indirect signal; no stated policy position.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 45

candidates.name: Kelabe Tewolde
candidates.summary: First-time candidate, Rainier Scholars leadership director, and Seattle Planning Commissioner running on 'a new generation of leadership,' with a platform shaped by childhood experience of housing insecurity and medical debt.
highlights: Challenger running explicitly on generational change against a roughly 27-year incumbent [S1][S3]
Seattle Planning Commissioner since 2020 and Associate Director of Leadership Development at Rainier Scholars — policy experience without prior elected office [S1]
Priorities include housing affordability, fully funding early childhood education, interest limits on medical debt, and community-based safety investment [S1][S2]

spending.basis: Calls for fully funding early childhood education and public investment in historically neglected areas like South Seattle and Skyway.

safety.basis: Advocates community-based safety built on public investment rather than enforcement-led approaches.

experience.basis: First-time candidate whose campaign message centers on 'a new generation of leadership' and lived experience.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 45

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton), voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat vacated by Rep. Chipalo Street's Senate run. Nonprofit executive Jaelynn Scott (D), heavily endorsed by Democratic officials, faces first-time Independent candidate Evon McCorkle, a college student with pamphlet-only evidence; both advance to the general.

candidates.name: Jaelynn Scott
candidates.summary: Executive Director of the Lavender Rights Project and community organizer with an extensive endorsement slate; would be the first openly transgender person to serve in the Washington Legislature.
highlights: Says she built a coalition that redirected $16 million from jail expansion into supportive housing and alternative public-safety approaches [S1][S2]

Platform includes 'compassionate public safety' (behavioral-health responders over police), universal healthcare, universal childcare, and progressive taxation on wealthy residents and corporations [S1][S2]
Endorsements span Rep. Pramila Jayapal, Sen. Bernie Sanders, King County Executive Girmay Zahilay, and numerous local Democratic officials [S1][S4]

taxes.basis: Calls for progressive taxation on wealthy residents and corporations and expanded cash assistance like the Working Families Tax Credit.

spending.basis: Platform of universal childcare, publicly funded preschool, and expanded cash assistance treats public investment as the primary lever.

housing.basis: Wants to expand affordable and permanent supportive housing on the Chief Seattle Club model, with a record of redirecting jail funds into supportive housing.

safety.basis: 'Compassionate public safety': behavioral-health professionals instead of police for crises, violence interruption funding, police accountability, and the $16M jail-to-housing redirection.

experience.basis: No prior elected office — an outsider by tenure — though with nonprofit executive leadership and a concrete claimed policy outcome.

immigration.basis: Supports limits on surveillance technology and data sharing with federal agencies.

healthcare.basis: Supports universal healthcare including vision, dental, and mental health coverage.

social.basis: Leads a nonprofit for Black gender-diverse communities and platforms protecting Medicaid, reproductive, and gender-affirming care.

tech.basis: Supports limits on surveillance technology and data sharing — a regulatory posture toward tech.

sources.ref: edition-1 pages 45-46

candidates.name: Evon McCorkle
candidates.summary: College student studying mathematics and physics and first-time candidate preferring the Independent Party; a lifelong district resident running on education access, housing stability, and restoring trust in government.
highlights: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience; states he is 'not backed by powerful insiders or political machines' [S1]
Lead priorities are education access, affordable housing and housing stability, and community safety [S1]
Evidence rests on the pamphlet alone: his campaign website was unreachable and no substantive news coverage was found [S1]

experience.basis: College student and first-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running as an outsider.

reform.basis: Frames his candidacy around restoring trust between residents and officials and being 'not backed by powerful insiders or political machines.'

sources.ref: edition-1 page 46

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Recently appointed-then-elected Democratic incumbent Janice Zahn holds a large resource and endorsement advantage over Republican Elle Nguyen, who offers the sole policy alternative on fiscal restraint and parental rights, and perennial candidate Alex Tsimerman, whose statement contains no policy content. The primary decides which challenger joins Zahn on the November ballot.

candidates.name: Janice Zahn
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat, civil engineer, and Port of Seattle Chief Engineer who served on the Bellevue City Council 2017-2025 and won the seat in a November 2025 special election after appointment.
highlights: Focused on 'improving affordability and delivering investments in housing, education, childcare, and transportation' [S1]
First-term bills signed into law span healthcare-provider accountability, public-works contracting, transportation safety, and a building-code change adding housing flexibility [S2][S3]
Vice-Chair of the House Local Government Committee; also sits on Housing, Finance, and Transportation [S1]
Says she aims to address state budget challenges 'responsibly' while protecting essential services [S1][S2]
Broad institutional endorsements: nurses and teachers unions, Planned Parenthood, Washington Conservation Action, Sierra Club, and sitting officials [S1][S2][S7]

spending.basis: Leads with delivering public investments in housing, education, childcare, and transportation, tempered by 'responsible' budget framing.

housing.basis: Frames housing as an investment priority, alongside a code-streamlining bill (HB 2228) adding supply flexibility.

climate.basis: Lists environmental protection among priorities and is endorsed by Washington Conservation Action and Sierra Club Washington.

experience.basis: Incumbent with a first-term bill record, eight years on the Bellevue council, and a career as Port of Seattle Chief Engineer.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood; equity- and access-oriented framing, without detailed social-policy statements in the dossier.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 54

sources.outlet: WA House Democrats official member page
PDC contributions dataset (data.wa.gov kv7h-kjye), filer_id ZAHNJ  005

candidates.name: Elle Nguyen
candidates.summary: Republican small business owner and law-school graduate from Mercer Island, making her third run for public office on fiscal restraint, public safety, and parental rights.
highlights: Priorities are 'stopping wasteful spending, balancing the budget, and protecting taxpayers from unnecessary tax increases' [S2]
Supports law enforcement and neighborhood safety [S1][S2]
Emphasizes parental rights, 'traditional values,' protecting children, and strengthening families [S1][S2]
Calls for open government, honest accounting, and accountability [S1][S2]
No reported fundraising or organizational endorsements found as of mid-July 2026 [S2][S5]

taxes.basis: Pledges to protect taxpayers from 'unnecessary tax increases' — restraint-oriented, though hedged rather than a blanket no-new-taxes stance.

spending.basis: Platform centers on stopping wasteful spending, balancing the budget, and honest accounting.

safety.basis: Supports law enforcement and neighborhood safety; no specific enforcement proposals in the dossier.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; runs on community advocacy after unsuccessful 2021 and 2022 bids.

social.basis: Campaigns on parental rights and 'traditional values,' the pamphlet and website's central family-policy theme.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 41st District (Bellevue, Mercer Island, Newcastle, parts of Renton, Issaquah, and Sammamish), voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat and House Deputy Majority Leader My-Linh Thai faces first-time Independent candidate Michael Rosen, an attorney and investor running on fiscal accountability; both advance to the general. The clearest divide is fiscal: protecting service investments versus spending restraint.

candidates.name: Michael Rosen
candidates.summary: Attorney, investor, and licensed nutritionist from Mercer Island; a first-time candidate preferring the Independent Party who is running on fiscal discipline and accountability for state spending.
highlights: Argues state spending 'more than doubled in two decades' while accountability, affordability, public safety, and health outcomes worsened; calls for fiscal discipline [S1]
Fourth-generation Washingtonian with a private-sector legal, investment, and nutrition background and no prior elected experience [S1][S3]
Describes himself as thinking independently and valuing 'outcomes over ideology'; his campaign site had no substantive policy content, so evidence rests on the pamphlet [S1][S2]

spending.basis: Core of his platform: state spending doubled while outcomes worsened; demands fiscal discipline and accountable treatment of tax dollars.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running on a private-sector background and independent thinking.

candidates.name: My-Linh T Thai
candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative since 2019 and House Deputy Majority Leader; a pharmacist and former Bellevue School Board President who describes herself as the first refugee elected to the Washington Legislature.
highlights: House Deputy Majority Leader with seats on eight committees including Appropriations and Finance [S1]
Top stated priorities are protecting access to affordable, high-quality healthcare and expanding behavioral health services [S1]
Former Bellevue School District Board President and Vice-President of the Washington State School Directors' Association [S1]

spending.basis: Emphasizes protecting care access and expanding behavioral health and education investment rather than spending restraint.

climate.basis: States support for 'meaningful action on climate change' and scientific innovation.

experience.basis: Seven-year incumbent with a House leadership post, broad committee assignments, and prior school-board leadership — a sustained governmental record.

healthcare.basis: Healthcare priorities framed as protecting access to affordable care and expanding behavioral health within existing structures; no universal-system platform.

contests.office_does: One of 49 members of the state Senate, elected to a four-year term to write state law, adopt state budgets, and set tax policy; the incumbent additionally leads the Senate majority caucus.
contests.race_blurb: Senate Majority Leader Jamie Pedersen, in the Legislature since 2007, faces a closely watched challenge from his left by first-time candidate and labor communicator Hannah Sabio-Howell, who won the 43rd District Democrats' and Seattle Mayor Katie Wilson's endorsements; scientist Heather-Marie Wilson, with no party preference and no reported fundraising, is the field's critic of recent tax increases.

candidates.name: Hannah Sabio-Howell
candidates.summary: First-time candidate and former Working Washington communications director and Senate Democratic Caucus staffer, challenging the Majority Leader from the left as a renter and movement organizer.
highlights: Treats the millionaires tax as a floor: wants a wealth tax and the Well Washington Fund payroll tax, and opposes estate-tax and luxury-tax rollbacks [S4][S5]
Backs statewide social housing, aggressive upzoning beyond HB 1110, and tighter rent stabilization, calling the 10% annual cap too high [S1][S4][S5]
Proposes a stepwise path to universal childcare, extending public education 'from zero to 12th grade' [S1][S4]
Wants to restore rolled-back police use-of-force and pursuit standards and to ban or limit ICE operations at hospitals, childcare centers, and courthouses [S4][S5]
Refuses corporate PAC money and won the 43rd District Democrats' and Seattle Mayor Katie Wilson's endorsements over the sitting Majority Leader [S1][S2][S6]

taxes.basis: Wants a wealth tax and payroll expense tax on top of the millionaires tax, with no rollbacks of estate or luxury taxes.

spending.basis: Platform of universal childcare, expanded Housing Trust Fund investment, and more state-funded transit service — public investment as the primary lever.

housing.basis: Backs statewide social housing, stronger Housing Trust Fund investment, and tighter rent stabilization than current law, alongside upzoning.

safety.basis: Wants to restore post-2020 police accountability standards that were rolled back and criticizes the $100 million police hiring incentive program as ineffective.

experience.basis: First-time candidate framing the race in generational and movement terms; would be the youngest state senator, with staff but no elected experience.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC money as a campaign centerpiece and supports structural changes like even-year local elections and a full-time Legislature.

immigration.basis: Wants to ban or limit ICE at sensitive locations, strip incentives from ICE contractors, and create pathways to sue ICE for constitutional violations.

healthcare.basis: Wants the state to backstop federal cuts and take steps toward universal healthcare, building on Universal Health Care Commission work.

social.basis: Serves on the Pro-Choice Washington PAC board; no further social-policy specifics in the dossier.

local-control.basis: Consistently favors statewide standards over local control: statewide upzoning mandates beyond HB 1110, a statewide social housing program, a tighter statewide rent cap, and state-mandated even-year local elections — pole_b's description names state housing mandates explicitly.

experience.basis: No prior elected experience; runs as a scientist-outsider rather than on a legislative program.

candidates.name: Jamie Pedersen
candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat and Senate Majority Leader since 2025, in the Legislature since 2007, a corporate general counsel and the lead architect of the state's new 9.9% millionaires income tax.
highlights: Architect of the 9.9% millionaires income tax and capital gains tax, paired with tax cuts for working families; supports rolling back the estate-tax increase and opposes a new payroll/wealth tax [S1][S6][S7]

Led Washington's marriage equality legislation and LGBTQ+ civil rights bills; restored state money to Planned Parenthood after federal cuts [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times, major SEIU locals, and every Democratic state senator, while the 43rd District Democrats and Seattle's mayor backed his challenger [S1][S2][S5][S6]

taxes.basis: Architect of the millionaires and capital gains taxes, but supports rolling back the estate-tax increase and opposes the Well Washington Fund payroll tax as infeasible — expansionist with limits.

spending.basis: Points to over $1 billion in new school funding and prefers rebalancing toward more public school investment; investment-oriented with a feasibility frame.

housing.basis: Cites past work on renter protections and a stated commitment to tackling housing affordability.

climate.basis: Cites climate action legislation among accomplishments and a Sierra Club endorsement; no detailed climate agenda in the dossier.

experience.basis: Twenty years in the Legislature, Senate Majority Leader, former Law & Justice chair, with a landmark enacted record.

reform.basis: Runs explicitly on insider effectiveness and coalition deal-making, accepts PAC money his opponent refuses, and serves as general counsel of a large construction/engineering firm.

immigration.basis: Enacted legislation banning ICE masking and restricting camera surveillance for immigration enforcement.

social.basis: Long record leading marriage equality, LGBTQ+ civil rights, and transgender privacy protections, plus restoring Planned Parenthood funding.

contests.office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, voting on state budgets, taxes, and laws covering schools, housing, healthcare, transportation, climate, and civil rights.

candidates.name: Vanessa Kritzer
candidates.summary: Redmond City Councilmember (2020–present) and Council President, and Executive Director of the Washington Association of Land Trusts, running on her municipal housing, tenant-protection, and climate record.
highlights: Six-plus years on the Redmond City Council, including as Council President; strengthened inclusionary zoning and led creation of a renter-protections code chapter [S4]
Supports progressive revenue including recent high-earner taxes and is open to broader structural reform such as an income tax [S4]
Record includes Redmond's climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and salmon recovery leadership [S4]; leads with climate, housing, and childcare [S1]
Holds the 45th District Democrats, WEA, State Labor Council, and most area electeds [S1]; leads fundraising with about $109K reported [S3]
Backed a Redmond resolution funding organizations serving immigrants and refugees; wants state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops [S4]

taxes.basis: Calls the tax code deeply regressive, supports high-earner taxes, and is open to structural income-tax reform and head-tax-style tools.

spending.basis: Platform centers public investment in housing, childcare, and climate; Seattle Times criticized her for naming no disagreement with her party's spending direction.

housing.basis: Led renter-protections code chapter, supports rent stabilization and permanent supportive housing, though paired with supply and zoning work.

climate.basis: Career conservation leadership plus a council record of climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and Climate Commitment Act funding.

experience.basis: The elected-record candidate in the race: six-plus years on the Redmond council with enacted housing and climate policy.

local-control.basis: Supports a stronger state role setting 'the floor' on housing production and calls affordability a regional/statewide problem.

immigration.basis: Backed a Redmond resolution affirming protections and funding for organizations serving immigrants and refugees.

social.basis: Pledges to defend civil rights with state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops; endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance.

candidates.name: Chandler Torbett
candidates.summary: A technology attorney and first-time candidate raised on the Muscogee Nation reservation, running on affordability, universal pre-K, AI consumer protections, and government accountability; endorsed by the Seattle Times.

Supports the million-dollar earners income tax and capital gains tax but says new taxes should come with broad-based relief [S4]
The only candidate centering AI regulation: wants consumer-protection guardrails on AI in hiring, healthcare, and finance while encouraging innovation [S4]

spending.basis: Runs on accountability and delivery — 'We've got to prove we can use this money wisely' — while still backing new programs like universal pre-K.

housing.basis: Emphasizes supply through zoning reform and density near transit; no tenant-protection or public-housing platform in the dossier.

climate.basis: Wants to accelerate the clean-energy transition, primarily by removing permitting and administrative obstacles.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, running on fresh execution and lived experience against a six-year councilmember.

tech.basis: Wants AI consumer-protection guardrails in hiring, healthcare, and financial decisions, balanced with encouraging innovation and jobs.

candidates.name: Gerry Pollet
candidates.summary: The incumbent since 2011, a public-interest attorney and UW public health faculty member who chairs the Legislature's audit committee (JLARC) and centers education funding and open government.

Cites passing 'ICE mask bans,' funding abortion access, and strengthening hate-crime laws [S1]
Backed by Gov. Ferguson, Rep. Jayapal, and labor, but outraised by both challengers this cycle [S6]

taxes.basis: Claims authorship-level roles on millionaires and capital gains taxes and advocates progressive tax reform to cut sales-tax reliance.

spending.basis: Chairs the Legislature's audit committee and pushes corporate tax-preference accountability while championing major education funding expansion.

housing.basis: Backs removing density barriers and transit-oriented development alongside inclusionary affordable-unit requirements — a blend of both poles.

climate.basis: Career environmental-cleanup leadership at Hanford, Washington Conservation Action endorsement, and microplastics-reduction legislation.

experience.basis: Seven-term incumbent with committee chairmanship, Appropriations seniority, and an enacted legislative record.

reform.basis: Long transparency record — open-government board service, public-records fights, and conflict-of-interest standards for legislators.

immigration.basis: Cites passing 'ICE mask bans' and protecting immigrants — matching the constrain-federal-enforcement pole.

social.basis: Funded abortion access, emphasizes reproductive freedom and LGBTQ+ rights, and strengthened hate-crime laws.

candidates.summary: A startup founder, housing-policy expert, and urbanist activist who narrowly lost a 2023 Seattle council race, backed by Seattle Mayor Katie Wilson and the housing movement.

Would tax wealthy individuals and corporations to fund schools, universal healthcare, and free childcare [S1]

spending.basis: Public investment is his primary lever: free childcare for all, transit investment, and funding universal public goods.

housing.basis: Spans both poles: supply-abundance urbanism (build enough homes) combined with social-housing advocacy and public-housing rent freezes.

experience.basis: Never held elected office and runs as a change candidate, though with a movement record of coalition wins and a prior council campaign.

reform.basis: Makes campaign-money independence an issue, attacking Dreher's AI-industry and out-of-state funding; organized grassroots corporate-taxation campaigns.

healthcare.basis: Lists funding 'universal healthcare' among the public goods his tax platform would pay for.

tech.basis: Prioritizes protecting consumers and workers from AI-related harms, a plank added after Dreher entered the race.

candidates.name: Will Dreher
candidates.summary: A first-time candidate and former federal prosecutor (January 6th cases, Kagan clerkship) making aggressive AI, data-center, and social-media regulation his campaign's centerpiece; endorsed by The Stranger.
highlights: The only candidate promising to 'aggressively regulate tech' — AI disinformation, automation job loss, data-center growth, and social-media safeguards for kids [S1]
Former Assistant U.S. Attorney who prosecuted about 25 January 6th defendants, hate crimes, and civil-rights violations by officers; clerked for Justice Kagan [S1]
Race's top fundraiser (~$263K); roughly $100K traces to AI-industry donors, which opponents cite to question his independence [S4]
Former 4th-grade teacher proposing to fully fund schools; would fix the regressive tax structure while holding government accountable for tax dollars [S1]
Endorsed by The Stranger [S6], Seattle City Attorney Erika Evans, and Councilmember Dionne Foster [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to fix Washington's regressive tax structure, without the structural-tax specifics his rivals offer.

housing.basis: Frames housing as both cutting red tape and funding affordable housing — brief pamphlet statements pointing to both poles.

safety.basis: Career prosecutor promising 'evidence-based public safety' and to hold criminals accountable; endorsed by a former mayoral public-safety chief.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no legislative or elected experience who entered in April 2026 and runs on a fresh professional lens.

tech.basis: Signature issue: aggressive AI, data-center, and social-media regulation as the centerpiece of his platform.

spending.basis: His pamphlet explicitly pairs both poles: 'hold government accountable for tax dollars' (audit/restrain) alongside fully funding schools and funding affordable housing, childcare, and transit (invest/expand). The scoring file's own highlight quotes this language yet no spending score was assigned; comparable mixed evidence earned Pollet a 0/medium.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 53
PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), queried 2026-07-16

sources.outlet: Washington State Standard (via Yakima Herald-Republic syndication)

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 46 (northeast Seattle); writes and votes on state law and the state operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. A Democratic incumbent with passed bills and committee leadership faces a first-time independent whose core message is rejecting party-line politics.

candidates.name: Darya Farivar
candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative and disability-equity specialist with a legislative record on food access, caregiver workforce, and disability policy.
highlights: Passed HB 2294 banning covenants that block new grocery stores and pharmacies, and HB 1395 removing caregiver employment barriers [S3][S4]
First Middle Eastern woman elected to the Washington Legislature; Vice Chair of Civil Rights & Judiciary [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the 46th District Democrats, Pro-Choice Washington, WA State Labor Council, AG Nick Brown, and Rep. Pramila Jayapal [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent with committee leadership roles and multiple bills passed, facing a first-time opponent.

social.basis: Endorsed by Pro-Choice Washington; civil-rights committee leadership and equity-focused career.

safety.basis: Board member of the Washington Coalition for Police Accountability; no direct policy statements in the dossier.

sources.ref: edition-1 page 54

candidates.name: Rodney 'Star' Thornley
candidates.summary: First-time candidate preferring the 'Independent Party' whose central message is constituent responsiveness over party-line politics.
highlights: 35 years with the Lake Washington Education Association; long record in sports officiating and water-ski organizations [S1]
Supports 'Washington State citizenship for undocumented residents' and a three-year college path [S1]
Runs as an independent because 'Olympia needs a culture change'; calls for fiscal accountability before spending [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected experience, running against a sitting legislator.

reform.basis: Runs as an independent against party-line politics, arguing officials follow party leadership rather than constituents.

immigration.basis: Supports 'Washington State citizenship for undocumented residents.'

spending.basis: Calls for fiscal accountability and 'a government that listens to citizens before it spends their money.'

sources.ref: edition-1 page 54

sources.outlet: WA Secretary of State, Amended 2026 Certification of Candidates to Primary Election

contests.office_does: One of 49 state senators; writes and votes on state law, sets state budgets, and confirms gubernatorial appointments for a four-year term.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. The Democratic incumbent, a committee chair, faces a Republican Covington city councilmember; both center affordability, housing supply, and public safety, differing mainly on means.

candidates.name: Claudia Kauffman
candidates.summary: Incumbent senator (2022-present, previously 2007-2011) and chair of the Senate Business, Trade & Economic Development Committee; describes herself as the first Native American woman elected to the Washington State Senate.
highlights: Top stated priority is 'making Washington affordable for everyone': housing supply plus protecting renters from 'predatory price gouging' [S1]
Cites childcare-access expansion, senior property-tax relief, and infrastructure funding for Highway 18 and SR 516 [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the mayors of Auburn, Covington, and Kent, County Executive Zahilay, and the WA State Labor Council [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent senator with a committee chairmanship and prior Senate service (2007-2011), versus a municipal-level challenger.

housing.basis: Pairs increased housing supply with renter protections against 'predatory price gouging.'

spending.basis: Emphasizes state investment: childcare expansion, a robust social safety net, and infrastructure funding she cites securing.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 61

candidates.name: Kristina Soltys
candidates.summary: Covington city councilmember since 2019 and Ukrainian immigrant challenging the incumbent from a tax-opposition and spending-restraint platform.
highlights: Opposes recent B&O and gas-tax increases and an income tax; says the state has 'a spending problem rather than a revenue shortage' [S2]
Favors housing supply through permitting reform and market incentives rather than state mandates [S2]
On the council: launched the 'Clean Team' program, supported full police funding, and helped pioneer a community resource officer program [S1]

experience.basis: Challenger with municipal council experience since 2019 but no state legislative record.

taxes.basis: Opposes recent B&O and gas-tax increases and an income tax; frames the state as having a spending problem, not a revenue shortage.

spending.basis: Platform stresses accountability and spending restraint over new state programs.

housing.basis: Favors increasing supply through permitting reform and market incentives rather than state mandates.

safety.basis: Supports fully funded law enforcement and more officers, paired with addiction-treatment resources and a community resource officer program.

local-control.basis: Opposes state housing mandates in favor of local permitting reform and incentives.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 61

contests.office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, writing and voting on state law and budgets covering school funding, housing, taxes, public safety, and healthcare.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Debra Entenman faces three challengers in a South King County swing district: Democratic newcomer Jasnoor Kaur Hans, self-styled 'Cascade Democrat' Logan Evans, and Libertarian Cobi Clark, who is endorsed by the district's Republicans. Public safety and addiction policy is the field's sharpest split.

candidates.name: Jasnoor Kaur Hans
candidates.summary: A Kent-raised legal advocate and political newcomer working in family law, with disability and immigrant-advocacy experience, running on housing, root-causes public safety, and generational change.
highlights: Housing platform centers public tools: expand the state Housing Trust Fund, fund tenant right-to-counsel, and support community land trusts [S2]
The field's clearest root-causes public-safety candidate, explicitly rejecting sentencing increases in favor of housing, mental-health services, and youth programs [S2]
Legal-advocacy background with the Washington Autism Alliance and immigration case support for Kids in Need of Defense [S1]
Frames the race against 'career politicians'; top fundraiser in the race at about $37K, more than double the incumbent [S3]

taxes.basis: Says she will 'make the tax system fairer' — directional but unspecified.

spending.basis: Platform is investment-forward: trust-fund expansion, funded right-to-counsel, more school counselors and mental-health staff.

housing.basis: Explicit public-tools platform: Housing Trust Fund expansion, tenant right-to-counsel, and community land trusts.

safety.basis: Explicitly rejects increased sentencing in favor of root causes — stable housing, mental-health services, youth programs.

experience.basis: Political newcomer in her first race, running against 'career politicians' on generational change.

immigration.basis: Platform defends immigrant protections and language access; volunteer immigration case support with Kids in Need of Defense.

healthcare.basis: Focuses on lowering out-of-pocket costs and holding insurers and hospital pricing accountable within the current system.

social.basis: Platform defends reproductive freedom, civil rights, and due process.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 62
PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), queried 2026-07-16

candidates.name: Cobi Clark
candidates.summary: A Libertarian housing developer and construction project manager, endorsed by both his party and the 47th District Republicans, running on supply-side housing, fiscal restraint, and civil liberties.
highlights: Argues from developer experience that excessive regulation, rising taxes, and bureaucratic delay drive up housing costs [S1]
Runs on fiscal responsibility, accountable government, and continued opposition to a state income tax [S2]
Calls for abundant, reliable, affordable energy and ties energy costs to regulation [S2]
Endorsed by the 47th District Republicans and the Libertarian Party, likely consolidating the right-of-center vote with no Republican filed [S2]
Lost an Auburn City Council race in 2025; raised about $19.5K this cycle [S4]

taxes.basis: Continued opposition to any state income tax and a limited-government fiscal platform.

spending.basis: Fiscal responsibility and accountable government are core planks, organized under 'Enact, Reform, and Repeal.'

housing.basis: Supply-side deregulation from hands-on developer experience; blames regulation, taxes, and bureaucratic delay for housing costs.

climate.basis: Energy platform is abundance and affordability, tying energy costs to regulation; no clean-energy or carbon-pricing planks.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; a first-time legislative candidate after losing a 2025 Auburn council race.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 62
PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), queried 2026-07-16

candidates.name: Logan Evans
candidates.summary: A Kent- and Auburn-area public school teacher running under a self-defined 'Cascade Democrat' label as a results-first alternative, proposing high-rise transit housing and a mandatory-rehab three-strikes policy.
highlights: Proposes a 'three-strikes' policy with six months of mandatory rehab — the most coercive treatment stance in the field [S1]
Supports high-rise housing near light rail to lower housing costs [S1]
Defines 'Cascade Democrat' as 'actionable rational solutions that don't waste your tax dollars,' pairing compassion with competence [S2]
Teacher at an alternative public high school; volunteers with addiction-recovery and homeless-housing groups; endorsed by ATU Local 587 [S1]

spending.basis: Runs on efficient use of tax dollars and results-first governance — 'solutions that don't waste your tax dollars.'

housing.basis: Housing answer is dense market supply — high-rise development near light rail to lower costs — without public-tools planks.

safety.basis: Proposes a three-strikes framework with six months of mandatory rehab, the field's most coercive treatment stance.

experience.basis: First-time legislative candidate positioning against the incumbent; lost a 2025 Kent council primary.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 63

sources.note: site blocked direct fetch (HTTP 403); content summarized from search-engine index of the site

sources.ref: PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), queried 2026-07-16

candidates.name: Debra Jean Entenman
candidates.summary: The incumbent Democrat since 2018, a former congressional district director with signed legislation on public-safety funding and college-student basic needs, sitting on four House committees.
highlights: Prime sponsor of HB 2015 (2025), which her pamphlet says secured $100 million for public safety with a local-option tax [S4]
Prime sponsor of HB 1559 (2023), creating benefits navigators for college students' basic needs [S5]
Public-safety approach pairs funding with accountability: independent deadly-force investigations, anti-bias hiring standards, and social workers in crisis response [S2]
Would expand TANF, eviction-prevention assistance, childcare, and healthcare access [S2]
Outraised by all three challengers this cycle at about $16K [S6]

taxes.basis: Sponsored and passed a local-option tax to fund public safety — new revenue authority rather than tax limitation.

spending.basis: Platform expands safety-net programs — TANF, eviction prevention, childcare — as the primary lever.

housing.basis: Emphasizes eviction-prevention assistance and safety-net supports from her Housing Committee seat; no deregulation planks.

safety.basis: Pairs $100M in new public-safety funding with accountability measures and social-worker crisis response — a documented blend of both poles.

experience.basis: Eight years in the seat, verifiable signed legislation, four committee assignments, and prior congressional staff service.

tech.basis: Consumer data-rights platform: mandatory disclosure of data collection with consumer control and informed consent.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 63
PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), queried 2026-07-16

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 47 (Kent, Auburn, Covington, Maple Valley area); writes and votes on state law and the state budgets.
contests.race_blurb: A rematch of the 2024 general election: Democratic incumbent Chris Stearns, the House Speaker Pro Tempore, again faces Republican Ted Cooke, a party-organization activist and software professional without prior elected office. Both advance to the general.

candidates.name: Chris Stearns
candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative and House Speaker Pro Tempore, a Navajo attorney with roughly 30 years working on Native American rights and multiple bills signed into law.
highlights: Bills signed into law include HB 1461 (clean energy infrastructure), a tribal apprenticeship bill, and HB 1681 doubling problem-gambling funds [S3]
Describes reproductive and gender-affirming care as human rights [S1][S2]
Speaker Pro Tempore with prior Auburn City Council service and a Clinton-era federal appointment; broad labor and Democratic endorsement slate [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Incumbent with House leadership roles, bills signed into law, and prior council, commission, and federal service.

housing.basis: Cites expanding affordable housing through state programs as a delivered result and continued priority.

spending.basis: Runs on delivered state investments in affordable housing, behavioral health, childcare, and nutrition.

climate.basis: Sponsored HB 1461 on clean energy infrastructure, signed into law; supports clean-energy job expansion.

social.basis: Describes reproductive and gender-affirming care as human rights, repeated across pamphlet and campaign site.

safety.basis: Prioritizes behavioral-health investment; served on the King County Mental Illness and Drug Dependency Committee.

tech.basis: Sponsored HB 1221 on data privacy and security, which passed the Senate.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 64
Official House Democrats caucus page for Rep. Stearns (bill signings) and leg.wa.gov member page

candidates.name: Ted Cooke
candidates.summary: Republican challenger and senior software tester with King County GOP organizational experience; he lost to Stearns in the 2024 general and runs on tax elimination, state audits, and parental rights.
highlights: Would freeze all new taxes, eliminate taxes on income, property, and fuel, and order audits of every state office and program [S1]
Would replace DEI with merit in government hiring, 'uphold the biological definition of gender,' and restore 'full parental rights in education' [S1][S2]
Prioritizes 'starter homes with yards' over high-density apartments he characterizes as forced on the district by Olympia [S1]

experience.basis: No prior elected public office; runs as an outsider against government overreach after losing to the same incumbent in 2024.

taxes.basis: Would freeze all new taxes and eliminate existing taxes on income, property, and fuel.

spending.basis: Would order audits of every state office and program, cut waste, and end commissions he characterizes as unaccountable.

housing.basis: Prioritizes market single-family 'starter homes with yards' and opposes state-driven density; no tenant-protection or public-housing agenda.

safety.basis: Names public safety a top priority and supports funding police to secure neighborhoods, parks, and schools.

social.basis: Would remove DEI from government hiring, 'uphold the biological definition of gender,' and restore 'full parental rights in education.'

local-control.basis: Characterizes density as forced on the district by Olympia and describes state government as overreaching in regulation and mandates.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 64

contests.office_does: One of 49 state senators; serves a four-year term voting on state budgets, taxes, and all state law.
contests.race_blurb: Uncontested: incumbent Sen. Vandana Slatter, appointed in January 2025 and retained in a November 2025 special election, is the only candidate and advances to the general regardless of the primary result.

candidates.name: Vandana Slatter
candidates.summary: Incumbent senator and licensed clinical pharmacist with 20+ years in biotechnology, previously a four-term state representative and Bellevue city councilmember.
highlights: Lead sponsor of the 'My Health My Data' Act (HB 1155) on consumer health data privacy and of a constitutional amendment protecting abortion rights [S1][S3]
Appointed to the Senate in January 2025 to succeed Patty Kuderer; retained the seat in the November 2025 special election [S3]
Priorities include affordable healthcare, reproductive freedom, support for immigrants, and a 'tax code that is fair to working people' [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent senator with an 8+ year legislative record (four House terms then Senate) plus prior city council service.

taxes.basis: Calls for a 'tax code that is fair to working people.'

housing.basis: Says she voted for affordable housing; self-reported vote with no detail.

climate.basis: Says she voted for climate action; endorsed by conservation voters.

immigration.basis: States support for immigrants and countering 'harmful federal overreach.'

healthcare.basis: Focus on affordable healthcare, keeping hospitals open, and medical-debt reduction within existing structures.

social.basis: Lead sponsor of a constitutional amendment protecting abortion rights; reproductive freedom is a stated priority; Planned Parenthood endorsement.

tech.basis: Lead sponsor of the My Health My Data Act, a consumer health data privacy law.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 65

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 48 (Redmond, Bellevue, Kirkland area); serves a two-year term voting on state budgets, taxes, and all state law.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. An appointed Democratic incumbent with broad establishment endorsements faces a Republican retired CPA running an almost entirely fiscal platform.

candidates.name: Osman Salahuddin
candidates.summary: Appointed to the House in January 2025 after serving on the Redmond City Council; Redmond native with a research and community-engagement background.
highlights: Says he secured childcare and healthcare investments and 'passed more laws than any legislator this year,' citing bills on affordable housing, gun-violence prevention, and immigrant protections [S1]
Former Redmond city councilmember and UW student body president; appointed to succeed Vandana Slatter [S1][S3]
Endorsed by Rep. DelBene, Gov. Ferguson, the WA State Labor Council, and teachers and nurses unions [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Appointed incumbent with about 1.5 years of legislative record plus prior city council service — a record case, but a short one.

spending.basis: Campaigns on secured investments in childcare, healthcare, housing, and education.

housing.basis: Cites bills on affordable housing (candidate's own characterization); public-investment framing.

immigration.basis: Cites passed bills on immigrant protections (candidate's own characterization).

climate.basis: Lists climate action among priorities; endorsed by Washington Conservation Action.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance; no direct statements in the dossier.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 66
WA House Democrats appointment announcement

candidates.name: Jeffery Poppe
candidates.summary: Retired CPA and financial systems consultant with 40 years of auditing experience, making his first run for state office on a fiscal-accountability platform.
highlights: Pledges to reject new taxes: Washington 'has a spending problem, not an income problem' [S1][S2]
Pledges to spearhead fraud audits and make state spending and performance metrics publicly accessible [S1][S2]
Accepts only individual contributions — no PAC or union money [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time state candidate with no prior elected public office; runs on an outside auditor's profile.

taxes.basis: Pledges to reject new taxes, framing the state as having 'a spending problem, not an income problem.'

spending.basis: Platform centers fraud audits, efficiency, and public spending/performance transparency.

reform.basis: Pledges to accept only individual contributions — no PAC or union money.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 66

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 48 (Redmond, Bellevue, Kirkland area); serves a two-year term voting on state budgets, taxes, and all state law.
contests.race_blurb: A Democrat-vs-Democrat contest: incumbent Rep. Amy Walen, a self-described pragmatic Democrat in the moderate/business lane, is challenged from the progressive/labor lane by Redmond City Councilmember Jessica Forsythe. Both advance to the general.

candidates.name: Jessica Forsythe
candidates.summary: Redmond city councilmember since 2020 and past council president challenging the incumbent from the progressive/labor lane, with the local Democratic-organization and state labor council endorsements.
highlights: Cites winning tenant protections and securing $7.5M for housing and human services on the Redmond council [S1][S2]
Co-authored Redmond's Climate Emergency Declaration; chaired the council's Alternative Crisis Response Subcommittee [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the 48th District Democrats, King County Democrats, WA State Labor Council, and Sen. Vandana Slatter [S1]

experience.basis: Challenger with a municipal record (Redmond council since 2020) but no state legislative record, running against a 7-year incumbent.

housing.basis: Cites winning tenant protections and securing $7.5M for housing and human services; campaigns on renter protections.

climate.basis: Co-authored Redmond's Climate Emergency Declaration and its Environmental Sustainability Action Plan; campaigns on climate resiliency.

safety.basis: Chaired Redmond's Alternative Crisis Response Subcommittee.

spending.basis: Campaigns on equitable K-12 funding and public investment in housing, human services, and behavioral health.

social.basis: Campaigns on reproductive rights; leads Emerge WA training women and non-binary leaders; Planned Parenthood endorsement.

sources.ref: edition-2 page 67

sources.note: accessed via search excerpt; direct fetch blocked

candidates.name: Amy Walen
candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative since 2019, chair of the House Consumer Protection & Business Committee, and former Kirkland mayor who self-describes as a 'pragmatic governing Democrat.'
highlights: Says she voted no on 'the income tax' and champions balanced budgets and fiscal responsibility [S1]
Backs a 'balanced approach' to public safety: community-focused law enforcement plus expanded mental health and crisis response; endorsed by the Bellevue Police Guild [S1][S2]
Cites votes banning ICE from wearing masks and backfilling Planned Parenthood funding [S1]

experience.basis: House member since 2019 with a committee chairmanship, plus a decade in Kirkland city government including the mayoralty.

taxes.basis: Highlights her no vote on 'the income tax' (her framing of the capital gains tax) and tax relief for families and small businesses.

spending.basis: Emphasizes balanced budgets, fiscal responsibility, and combating wasteful spending, citing the regional homelessness authority.

housing.basis: Supports both housing investment and missing-middle supply reform; no tenant-protection emphasis — mixed on this axis.

safety.basis: Explicitly 'balanced approach': investment in community-focused law enforcement (Bellevue Police Guild endorsement) alongside expanded mental health and crisis response.

climate.basis: Supports clean energy and low-carbon fuel standards.

immigration.basis: Cites her vote to ban ICE from wearing masks.

social.basis: Prioritizes protecting reproductive healthcare access; cites backfilling Planned Parenthood funding.
